<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The build now fails on **mamba-ssm==1.0.1**, not causal-conv1d. The same bug: `bare_metal_version` is not defined when nvcc is not found (setup.py line 111). So mamba-ssm also has a setup.py that only sets `bare_metal_version` when CUDA_HOME is not None, but then uses it unconditionally.

I need to either:
1. Set an env var for mamba-ssm to skip CUDA build (if it has one like causal-conv1d)
2. Or patch the SUM requirements so we install mamba-ssm in a way that avoids the setup.py bug

Let me search for mamba-ssm skip cuda build or MAMBA_SSM env.
</details></think>

[Search code, repositories, users, issues, pull requests...](https://github.com/state-spaces/mamba/issues/589)\n\n# Mamba SSM Setup.py CUDA Build Configuration\n\n## Skip CUDA Build Environment Variable\n\nTo skip CUDA compilation during mamba-ssm installation, set the &#96;MAMBA_SKIP_CUDA_BUILD&#96; environment variable:[1]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nMAMBA_SKIP_CUDA_BUILD=TRUE python setup.py sdist\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThis environment variable is intended to allow CI systems to run &#96;python setup.py sdist&#96; without any CUDA compilation, simply copying raw files instead.[1]\n\n## Related Configuration Options\n\nThe setup.py also supports other build control variables:[1]\n- &#96;MAMBA_FORCE_BUILD&#96;: Force a fresh local build instead of attempting to find prebuilt wheels\n- &#96;MAMBA_FORCE_CXX11_ABI&#96;: Build with C++11 ABI (useful for NVCR images that use C++11 ABI)\n\n## Common Installation Issues\n\nA frequent error occurs when &#96;nvcc&#96; (NVIDIA CUDA Compiler) is not found, which can cause &#96;bare_metal_version&#96; to be undefined during setup.[3][4][5] This typically happens when:\n- Installing without CUDA development tools\n- Using PyTorch containers that don't include development tools (non-'devel' images)\n\nUsers experiencing build issues are advised to download pre-built wheels matching their specific environment (CUDA version, PyTorch version, and ABI compatibility) from the [GitHub releases page](https://github.com/state-spaces/mamba/releases) rather than building from source.[2]",

```json
{
  "markdown": "# Copyright (c) 2023, Albert Gu, Tri Dao.\nimport sys\nimport warnings\nimport os\nimport re\nimport ast\nfrom pathlib import Path\nfrom packaging.version import parse, Version\nimport platform\n\nfrom setuptools import setup, find_packages\nimport subprocess\n\nimport urllib.request\nimport urllib.error\nfrom wheel.bdist_wheel import bdist_wheel as _bdist_wheel\n\nimport torch\nfrom torch.utils.cpp_extension import (\n BuildExtension,\n CppExtension,\n CUDAExtension,\n CUDA_HOME,\n)\n\n\nwith open(\"README.md\", \"r\", encoding=\"utf-8\") as fh:\n long_description = fh.read()\n\n\n# ninja build does not work unless include_dirs are abs path\nthis_dir = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))\n\nPACKAGE_NAME = \"mamba_ssm\"\n\nBASE_WHEEL_URL = \"https://github.com/state-spaces/mamba/releases/download/{tag_name}/{wheel_name}\"\n\n# FORCE_BUILD: Force a fresh build locally, instead of attempting to find prebuilt wheels\n# SKIP_CUDA_BUILD: Intended to allow CI to use a simple &#96;python setup.py sdist&#96; run to copy over raw files, without any cuda compilation\nFORCE_BUILD = os.getenv(\"MAMBA_FORCE_BUILD\", \"FALSE\") == \"TRUE\"\nSKIP_CUDA_BUILD = os.getenv(\"MAMBA_SKIP_CUDA_BUILD\", \"FALSE\") == \"TRUE\"\n# For CI, we want the option to build with C++11 ABI since the nvcr images use C++11 ABI\nFORCE_CXX11_ABI = os.getenv(\"MAMBA_FORCE_CXX11_ABI\", \"FALSE\") == \"TRUE\"\n\n\ndef get_platform():\n \"\"\"\n Returns the platform name as used in wheel filenames.\n \"\"\"\n if sys.platform.startswith(\"linux\"):\n return \"linux_x86_64\"\n elif sys.platform == \"darwin\":\n mac_version = \".\".join(platform.mac_ver()[0].split(\".\")[:2])\n return f\"macosx_{mac_version}_x86_64\"\n elif sys.platform == \"win32\":\n return \"win_amd64\"\n else:\n raise ValueError(\"Unsupported platform: {}\".format(sys.platform))\n\n\ndef get_cuda_bare_metal_version(cuda_dir):\n raw_output = subprocess.check_output(\n [cuda_dir + \"/bin/nvcc\", \"-V\"], universal_newlines=True\n )\n output = raw_output.split()\n release_idx = output.index(\"release\") + 1\n bare_metal_version = parse(output[release_idx].split(\",\")[0])\n\n return raw_output, bare_metal_version\n\n\ndef check_if_cuda_home_none(global_option: str) -\u003e None:\n if CUDA_HOME is not None:\n return\n # warn instead of error because user could be downloading prebuilt wheels, so nvcc won't be necessary\n # in that case.\n warnings.warn(\n f\"{global_option} was requested, but nvcc was not found. Are you sure your environment has nvcc available? \"\n \"If you're installing within a container from https://hub.docker.com/r/pytorch/pytorch, \"\n \"only images whose names contain 'devel' will provide nvcc.\"\n )\n\n\ndef append_nvcc_threads(nvcc_extra_args):\n return nvcc_extra_args + [\"--threads\", \"4\"]\n\n\ncmdclass = {}\next_modules = []\n\nif not SKIP_CUDA_BUILD:\n print(\"\\n\\ntorch.__version__ = {}\\n\\n\".format(torch.__version__))\n TORCH_MAJOR = int(torch.__version__.split(\".\")[0])\n TORCH_MINOR = int(torch.__version__.split(\".\")[1])\n\n check_if_cuda_home_none(PACKAGE_NAME)\n # Check, if CUDA11 is installed for compute capability 8.0\n cc_flag = []\n if CUDA_HOME is not None:\n _, bare_metal_version = get_cuda_bare_metal_version(CUDA_HOME)\n if bare_metal_version \u003c Version(\"11.6\"):\n raise RuntimeError(\n f\"{PACKAGE_NAME} is only supported on CUDA 11.6 and above. \"\n \"Note: make sure nvcc has a supported version by running nvcc -V.\"\n )\n\n cc_flag.append(\"-gencode\")\n cc_flag.append(\"arch=compute_70,code=sm_70\")\n cc_flag.append(\"-gencode\")\n cc_flag.append(\"arch=compute_80,code=sm_80\")\n if bare_metal_version \u003e= Version(\"11.8\"):\n cc_flag.append(\"-gencode\")\n cc_flag.append(\"arch=compute_90,code=sm_90\")\n\n # HACK: The compiler flag -D_GLIBCXX_USE_CXX11_ABI is set to be the same as\n # torch._C._GLIBCXX_USE_CXX11_ABI\n # https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/8472c24e3b5b60150096486616d98b7bea01500b/torch/utils/cpp_extension.py#L920\n if FORCE_CXX11_ABI:\n torch._C._GLIBCXX_USE_CXX11_ABI = True\n\n ext_modules.append(\n CUDAExtension(\n name=\"selective_scan_cuda\",\n sources=[\n \"csrc/selective_scan/selective_scan.cpp\",\n \"csrc/selective_scan/selective_scan_fwd_fp32.cu\",\n \"csrc/selective_scan/selective_scan_fwd_fp16.cu\",\n \"csrc/selective_scan/selective_scan_fwd_bf16.cu\",\n \"csrc/selective_scan/selective_scan_bwd_fp32_real.cu\",\n \"csrc/selective_scan/selective_scan_bwd_fp32_complex.cu\",\n \"csrc/selective_scan/selective_scan_bwd_fp16_real.cu\",\n \"csrc/selective_scan/selective_scan_bwd_fp16_complex.cu\",\n \"csrc/selective_scan/selective_scan_bwd_bf16_real.cu\",\n \"csrc/selective_scan/selective_scan_bwd_bf16_complex.cu\",\n ],\n extra_compile_args={\n \"cxx\": [\"-O3\", \"-std=c++17\"],\n \"nvcc\": append_nvcc_threads(\n [\n \"-O3\",\n \"-std=c++17\",\n \"-U__CUDA_NO_HALF_OPERATORS__\",\n \"-U__CUDA_NO_HALF_CONVERSIONS__\",\n \"-U__CUDA_NO_BFLOAT16_OPERATORS__\",\n \"-U__CUDA_NO_BFLOAT16_CONVERSIONS__\",\n \"-U__CUDA_NO_BFLOAT162_OPERATORS__\",\n \"-U__CUDA_NO_BFLOAT162_CONVERSIONS__\",\n \"--expt-relaxed-constexpr\",\n \"--expt-extended-lambda\",\n \"--use_fast_math\",\n \"--ptxas-options=-v\",\n \"-lineinfo\",\n ]\n + cc_flag\n ),\n },\n include_dirs=[Path(this_dir) / \"csrc\" / \"selective_scan\"],\n )\n )\n\n\ndef get_package_version():\n with open(Path(this_dir) / PACKAGE_NAME / \"__init__.py\", \"r\") as f:\n version_match = re.search(r\"^__version__\\s*=\\s*(.*)$\", f.read(), re.MULTILINE)\n public_version = ast.literal_eval(version_match.group(1))\n local_version = os.environ.get(\"MAMBA_LOCAL_VERSION\")\n if local_version:\n return f\"{public_version}+{local_version}\"\n else:\n return str(public_version)\n\n\ndef get_wheel_url():\n # Determine the version numbers that will be used to determine the correct wheel\n # We're using the CUDA version used to build torch, not the one currently installed\n # _, cuda_version_raw = get_cuda_bare_metal_version(CUDA_HOME)\n torch_cuda_version = parse(torch.version.cuda)\n torch_version_raw = parse(torch.__version__)\n # For CUDA 11, we only compile for CUDA 11.8, and for CUDA 12 we only compile for CUDA 12.2\n # to save CI time. Minor versions should be compatible.\n torch_cuda_version = parse(\"11.8\") if torch_cuda_version.major == 11 else parse(\"12.2\")\n python_version = f\"cp{sys.version_info.major}{sys.version_info.minor}\"\n platform_name = get_platform()\n mamba_ssm_version = get_package_version()\n # cuda_version = f\"{cuda_version_raw.major}{cuda_version_raw.minor}\"\n cuda_version = f\"{torch_cuda_version.major}{torch_cuda_version.minor}\"\n torch_version = f\"{torch_version_raw.major}.{torch_version_raw.minor}\"\n cxx11_abi = str(torch._C._GLIBCXX_USE_CXX11_ABI).upper()\n\n # Determine wheel URL based on CUDA version, torch version, python version and OS\n wheel_filename = f\"{PACKAGE_NAME}-{mamba_ssm_version}+cu{cuda_version}torch{torch_version}cxx11abi{cxx11_abi}-{python_version}-{python_version}-{platform_name}.whl\"\n wheel_url = BASE_WHEEL_URL.format(\n tag_name=f\"v{mamba_ssm_version}\", wheel_name=wheel_filename\n )\n return wheel_url, wheel_filename\n\n\nclass CachedWheelsCommand(_bdist_wheel):\n \"\"\"\n The CachedWheelsCommand plugs into the default bdist wheel, which is ran by pip when it cannot\n find an existing wheel (which is currently the case for all installs). We use\n the environment parameters to detect whether there is already a pre-built version of a compatible\n wheel available and short-circuits the standard full build pipeline.\n \"\"\"\n\n def run(self):\n if FORCE_BUILD:\n return super().run()\n\n wheel_url, wheel_filename = get_wheel_url()\n print(\"Guessing wheel URL: \", wheel_url)\n try:\n urllib.request.urlretrieve(wheel_url, wheel_filename)\n\n # Make the archive\n # Lifted from the root wheel processing command\n # https://github.com/pypa/wheel/blob/cf71108ff9f6ffc36978069acb28824b44ae028e/src/wheel/bdist_wheel.py#LL381C9-L381C85\n if not os.path.exists(self.dist_dir):\n os.makedirs(self.dist_dir)\n\n impl_tag, abi_tag, plat_tag = self.get_tag()\n archive_basename = f\"{self.wheel_dist_name}-{impl_tag}-{abi_tag}-{plat_tag}\"\n\n wheel_path = os.path.join(self.dist_dir, archive_basename + \".whl\")\n print(\"Raw wheel path\", wheel_path)\n os.rename(wheel_filename, wheel_path)\n except urllib.error.HTTPError:\n print(\"Precompiled wheel not found. Building from source...\")\n # If the wheel could not be downloaded, build from source\n super().run()\n\n\nsetup(\n name=PACKAGE_NAME,\n version=get_package_version(),\n packages=find_packages(\n exclude=(\n \"build\",\n \"csrc\",\n \"include\",\n \"tests\",\n \"dist\",\n \"docs\",\n \"benchmarks\",\n \"mamba_ssm.egg-info\",\n )\n ),\n author=\"Tri Dao, Albert Gu\",\n author_email=\"tri@tridao.me, agu@cs.cmu.edu\",\n description=\"Mamba state-space model\",\n long_description=long_description,\n long_description_content_type=\"text/markdown\",\n url=\"https://github.com/state-spaces/mamba\",\n classifiers=[\n \"Programming Language :: Python :: 3\",\n \"License :: OSI Approved :: BSD License\",\n \"Operating System :: Unix\",\n ],\n ext_modules=ext_modules,\n cmdclass={\"bdist_wheel\": CachedWheelsCommand, \"build_ext\": BuildExtension}\n if ext_modules\n else {\n \"bdist_wheel\": CachedWheelsCommand,\n },\n python_requires=\"\u003e=3.7\",\n install_requires=[\n \"torch\",\n \"packaging\",\n \"ninja\",\n \"einops\",\n \"triton\",\n \"transformers\",\n \"causal_conv1d\",\n ],\n)\n",
  "url": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/state-spaces/mamba/v1.0.1/setup.py"
}
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

mamba-ssm 1.0.1 has the same pattern: `MAMBA_SKIP_CUDA_BUILD` (not MAMBA_SSM_SKIP_CUDA_BUILD - the web search said MAMBA_SKIP_CUDA_BUILD). When SKIP_CUDA_BUILD is True, the block that uses bare_metal_version is skipped. So we need to set:
- MAMBA_SKIP_CUDA_BUILD=TRUE
- And likely MAMBA_FORCE_BUILD=TRUE so it doesn't try to download a prebuilt wheel (which would use torch.version.cuda and fail with CPU torch)

Adding these to the Dockerfile.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_


Erledigt. Der Fehler kam diesmal von **mamba-ssm**: gleicher `bare_metal_version`-Bug im setup.py, wenn kein nvcc vorhanden ist.

**Änderung im Dockerfile.sum:**
- **`MAMBA_SKIP_CUDA_BUILD=TRUE`** – unterbindet den CUDA-Build und damit die Zeile mit `bare_metal_version`.
- **`MAMBA_FORCE_BUILD=TRUE`** – verhindert den Download eines Prebuilt-Wheels (der mit CPU-PyTorch wegen `torch.version.cuda` fehlschlagen würde).

Die Knowledge-Datei ist um Punkt 4 (mamba-ssm) ergänzt.

Bitte erneut committen, pushen und in Coolify neu deployen. Wenn beim Installieren von mamba-ssm oder einer anderen SUM-Dependency wieder ein Fehler kommt, den Log posten.

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The container starts but crashes because OpenCV (cv2) needs libGL.so.1 - that's the OpenGL library. In a headless/server container we don't have a display, so we need either:
1. Install the libGL library (e.g. libgl1-mesa-glx or libgl1) so cv2 can load
2. Or use opencv-python-headless instead of opencv-python - the headless variant doesn't depend on GUI/GL

SUM's requirements.txt has opencv-python==4.6.0.66. The headless package is opencv-python-headless. So we have two options:
1. In the Dockerfile, after installing SUM requirements, install libgl1 (or libgl1-mesa-glx) via apt-get - quick fix
2. Patch SUM's requirements or override in Dockerfile to use opencv-python-headless instead of opencv-python - cleaner for server/container

The cleanest approach for a server container is opencv-python-headless (no GUI deps). We can't easily change SUM's requirements.txt since it's from the cloned repo. So we can:
- Either add a RUN step that does: pip uninstall -y opencv-python && pip install opencv-python-headless==4.6.0.66
- Or install libgl1 in the Dockerfile: apt-get install -y libgl1-mesa-glx or libgl1

opencv-python-headless is the better long-term solution for headless environments. Let me add both: install the headless variant after SUM requirements (and uninstall opencv-python if present), and optionally add libgl1 as a fallback. Actually just switching to headless is enough - we don't need libGL at all then.

Checking: opencv-python-headless 4.6.0.66 exists on PyPI. So in Dockerfile.sum after the SUM requirements install we do:
RUN pip uninstall -y opencv-python 2>/dev/null; pip install --no-cache-dir opencv-python-headless==4.6.0.66

That way we satisfy the version pin (4.6.0.66) but use the headless build.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user is getting a 502 Bad Gateway when POSTing to `/api/saliency/generate`. This typically means:

1. The CHECKION app receives the request but when it tries to proxy/forward to the saliency service (or when the saliency route tries to fetch the external saliency service), something fails - e.g. the saliency container is unreachable, crashed, or returns an error that gets translated to 502.
2. Or there's a reverse proxy (Coolify/nginx) in front of CHECKION that returns 502 when CHECKION takes too long or crashes.

Looking at the flow:
- Browser → POST https://checkion.projects-a.plygrnd.tech/api/saliency/generate
- That hits the CHECKION Next.js app
- The route handler in app/api/saliency/generate/route.ts fetches the saliency service (SALIENCY_SERVICE_URL) and waits for the response (up to 3 min timeout)
- If the saliency service is down, unreachable, or times out, the route could return 502 or the proxy could return 502 if the request takes too long

502 Bad Gateway usually means:
- The upstream (CHECKION's backend, or CHECKION calling the saliency service) failed or timed out
- Or the saliency service returned an error and the route returns 500, but the proxy might convert it to 502 in some setups

Let me check the saliency generate route to see what it returns on errors and if there's a timeout issue. Coolify/reverse proxies often have a default proxy read timeout (e.g. 60s). If the saliency computation takes 1-3 minutes on CPU, the proxy might close the connection before CHECKION responds, resulting in 502.

So the fix could be:
1. Increase the proxy/gateway timeout in Coolify for the CHECKION app (e.g. 180s or 300s) so long-running saliency requests don't get cut off.
2. Ensure SALIENCY_SERVICE_URL is correct and the saliency container is reachable from CHECKION.
3. Check saliency container logs to see if it's receiving the request and responding or crashing.

I'll suggest checking both: proxy timeout (very likely for CPU saliency taking 1-3 min) and SALIENCY_SERVICE_URL/reachability. I can also check if the route returns something that might be interpreted as 502 or if we need to document Coolify timeout settings.
</details></think>

```json
{
  "markdown": "Gateway Timeout Errors | Coolify Docs\n\n# Gateway Timeout (504) Errors ​\n\nGateway timeout errors occur when the Coolify proxy cannot get a response from your application within the configured timeout period. This is different from [Bad Gateway (502)](https://coolify.io/docs/troubleshoot/applications/bad-gateway#bad-gateway-502-error) errors, which indicate the proxy cannot connect to your application at all.\n\n## Common Causes ​\n\nThere are two primary scenarios that cause 504 Gateway Timeout errors in Coolify:\n\n1. Large Upload/Download Timeouts - Default timeout settings are too short for large file transfers\n2. Custom Docker Network Isolation - The proxy cannot reach applications using custom networks\n\n## Issue 1: Custom Docker Network Isolation ​\n\n### Symptoms ​\n\n- Using custom Docker networks in your configuration\n- Restarting the application temporarily fixes the issue\n- Application is reachable via direct IP and port (requires manual port mapping)\n- 504 Gateway Timeout errors appear after hours or days\n- Application works initially after deployment\n\n### Root Cause ​\n\nWhen you define custom Docker networks in your Docker Compose file, the&#96;coolify-proxy&#96; container runs in Coolify's own networks while your application runs in the custom network. This network isolation prevents the proxy from reaching your application, especially when Docker's internal DNS returns different IPs based on timing and network joins.\n\n### Diagnosis ​\n\nCheck if your application uses custom networks:\n\nbash\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\ndocker inspect \u003cyour-container-name\u003e --format='{{range $k,$v := .NetworkSettings.Networks}}Network: {{$k}}, IP: {{$v.IPAddress}}, Gateway: {{$v.Gateway}}{{println}}{{end}}'\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nVerify proxy network connections:\n\nbash\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\ndocker inspect coolify-proxy --format='{{range $k,$v := .NetworkSettings.Networks}}Network: {{$k}}, IP: {{$v.IPAddress}}, Gateway: {{$v.Gateway}}{{println}}{{end}}'\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Solutions ​\n\n#### Solution 1: Use Coolify Destinations (Recommended) ​\n\nLet Coolify manage networks automatically by using [Destinations](https://coolify.io/docs/knowledge-base/destinations/) instead of custom networks:\n\n1. Redeploy\n2. [Move your application / service to the desired destination](https://coolify.io/docs/knowledge-base/destinations/manage#assign-resources-to-a-destination)\n3. [Configure the network destination](https://coolify.io/docs/knowledge-base/destinations/create) in Coolify's UI under Destinations\n4. Remove custom network definitions from your Docker Compose file\n\nBefore (problematic):\n\nyaml\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nservices:\n  app:\n    image: myapp:latest\n    networks:\n      - custom-network\n\nnetworks:\n  custom-network:\n    driver: bridge\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nAfter (recommended):\n\nyaml\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nservices:\n  app:\n    image: myapp:latest\n    # Let Coolify handle networking\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n#### Solution 2: Manual Network Connection (Temporary) ​\n\nIf you must use custom networks, manually connect the proxy:\n\nbash\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\ndocker network connect \u003cyour-network-name\u003e coolify-proxy\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nNote: This is a temporary fix that may need to be reapplied after proxy restarts.\n\n## Issue 2: Large Upload/Download Timeouts ​\n\n### Symptoms ​\n\n- Small files and quick requests work fine\n- 504 errors during long-running requests (\u003e60 seconds for Traefik/Nginx)\n- 504 errors when pushing large Docker images to a registry\n- 504 errors when uploading large files (\u003e100MB)\n\n### Root Cause ​\n\nThe default timeout behavior depends on your proxy:\n\n- Nginx (one-click databases): Default timeout is 60 seconds\n- Caddy: No default timeout (requests can run indefinitely)\n- Traefik: Default read timeout is 60 seconds\n\nAny request exceeding the configured timeout will result in a 504 Gateway Timeout error, even if the backend application is still processing the request.\n\n### Diagnosis ​\n\nCheck which proxy you're using:\n\n- The proxy type (Traefik, Caddy, etc.) will be displayed\n- Navigate to Servers \u003e [YourServer] \u003e Proxy in the Coolify UI\n\nCheck current proxy configuration:\n\n- Check your applications / services for custom labels that might override defaults\n- Navigate to Servers \u003e [YourServer] \u003e Proxy and look for any timeout settings\n\nMonitor request duration in application logs:\n\n- Look for long-running requests that exceed 60 seconds\n- Navigate to your application / service logs in Coolify\n\nTest with a smaller file to confirm it's size-related\n\n### Solutions ​\n\n#### Solution 1: Increase Proxy Timeout ​\n\nThe configuration method depends on your proxy type:\n\n##### For Traefik (Default Proxy) ​\n\nAdd custom Traefik configuration to increase the timeout. You have various options depending on your needs to achieve this:\n\nNavigate to your server's proxy settings and add the new timeouts under the command section:\n\nyaml\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\ncommand:\n  - '--entrypoints.https.transport.respondingTimeouts.readTimeout=5m'\n  - '--entrypoints.https.transport.respondingTimeouts.writeTimeout=5m'\n  - '--entrypoints.https.transport.respondingTimeouts.idleTimeout=5m'\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nRead more about Traefik timeouts in the [official documentation](https://doc.traefik.io/traefik/reference/install-configuration/entrypoints/#timeout).\n\n##### For Caddy ​\n\nSince Caddy has no default timeout, you typically won't experience timeout issues. However, if you need to SET a timeout (for security or resource management):\n\n1. Add the following to your Container Labels in Coolify:\n\nyaml\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n# Set a 5-minute timeout (300 seconds)\ncaddy.servers.timeouts.read_body=300s\ncaddy.servers.timeouts.read_header=300s\ncaddy.servers.timeouts.write=300s\ncaddy.servers.timeouts.idle=5m\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nRead more about Caddy timeouts in the [official documentation](https://caddyserver.com/docs/caddyfile/options#timeouts).\n\n##### For Nginx (One-Click Databases) ​\n\nNginx configuration cannot be directly modified for one-click databases in Coolify. Instead, bypass Nginx entirely to avoid timeout issues:\n\n1. This maps the port directly from the container, bypassing Nginx and its timeout limitations\n2. Use Port Mappings instead to expose the database port directly and restart the database\n3. Disable \"Make it publicly available?\" option\n4. Navigate to your database settings in Coolify\n\nExample: For a PostgreSQL database, map port&#96;5432:5432&#96; to access it directly without any proxy timeouts.\n\nRead more about public database access here: [One-Click Databases](https://coolify.io/docs/databases/#ports-mapping-vs-public-port).\n\n#### Solution 2: Implement Chunked Uploads ​\n\nFor very large files, consider implementing chunked uploads in your application:\n\n1. Reassemble on the server side\n2. Upload chunks individually (each under the timeout limit)\n3. Split large files into smaller chunks on the client side\n\n#### Solution 3: Use Background Processing ​\n\nFor long-running operations:\n\n1. Provide an endpoint to check job status\n2. Process the request in the background\n3. Accept the request and return immediately with a job ID\n\n## Quick Diagnosis Checklist ​\n\nRun through these steps to identify your specific issue:\n\nCheck the error code:\n\n- 502 = Bad Gateway (see [Bad Gateway troubleshooting](https://coolify.io/docs/troubleshoot/applications/bad-gateway))\n- 504 = Gateway Timeout (this guide)\n\nCheck timing:\n\n- Random after hours/days = likely network isolation issue\n- After ~60 seconds = likely timeout issue\n- Immediate = likely network/configuration issue\n\nCheck network configuration:\n\nbash\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n# List all Docker networks\ndocker network ls\n\n# Check which networks your containers are using\ndocker ps --format \"table {{.Names}}\\t{{.Networks}}\"\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Prevention Tips ​\n\n1. Use progress indicators for long-running operations to prevent client-side timeouts\n2. Monitor proxy logs regularly for timeout patterns\n3. Implement health checks to maintain connectivity\n4. Set appropriate timeouts for your application's needs during initial setup\n5. Avoid custom Docker networks unless absolutely necessary\n\n## Support ​\n\nIf these solutions don't resolve your gateway timeout issues:\n\nCollect diagnostic information:\n\nbash\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n# Save this output\ndocker ps --format \"table {{.Names}}\\t{{.Networks}}\\t{{.Status}}\"\ndocker logs coolify-proxy --tail 200 \u003e proxy-logs.txt\ndocker logs \u003cyour-container-name\u003e --tail 200 \u003e app-logs.txt\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nJoin our [Discord Community](https://coolify.io/discord)\n\nShare your configuration, logs, and the specific steps you've tried",
  "url": "https://coolify.io/docs/troubleshoot/applications/gateway-timeout"
}
```
User decision: **accepted**

Status: **completed**

</tool-use>

So Coolify says:
- 502 = Bad Gateway = proxy cannot connect to the application at all
- 504 = Gateway Timeout = proxy didn't get response within timeout

The user got 502. So it could be either:
1. Proxy can't connect to CHECKION (unlikely if the rest of the app works)
2. Or the proxy is cutting the connection and returning 502 in some setups when the backend takes too long (some proxies return 502 instead of 504 when the upstream connection is closed)
3. Or CHECKION is returning 502 because the saliency service is unreachable (our route returns 502 on fetch failure)

So we have two angles:
1. **Increase proxy timeout** – so that the proxy doesn't cut the request before CHECKION gets the saliency response (e.g. after 60s). This is needed for Traefik: add readTimeout=5m etc.
2. **Ensure SALIENCY_SERVICE_URL** – so CHECKION can reach the saliency container. If it can't, our route returns 502.

I'll update the doc to add a dedicated "502 bei /api/saliency/generate" section that covers:
- Proxy-Timeout (Traefik 60s): Zeit erhöhen (z. B. 5m) unter Server > Proxy > Configuration
- SALIENCY_SERVICE_URL prüfen, Saliency-Logs prüfen

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user is saying that the SUM saliency service actually has the domain https://sum-saliency.projects-a.plygrnd.tech. So they're accessing it via a public domain, not via the internal Docker hostname.

For CHECKION (running in Coolify) to call the saliency service, it has two options:
1. **Internal URL**: Use the internal container name/hostname (e.g. http://container-id:8000) - no TLS, same network, no proxy timeout for this internal call.
2. **Public domain URL**: Use https://sum-saliency.projects-a.plygrnd.tech - then CHECKION would make an outbound HTTPS request to that domain. The request would go: CHECKION container → Coolify proxy / internet → sum-saliency domain → proxy → SUM container. That can work, but (a) the proxy in front of CHECKION still has a 60s timeout for the *client* request (browser → CHECKION), and (b) if they use the public URL, CHECKION would need to wait for the full round trip; the 502 they saw could still be the proxy in front of CHECKION timing out after 60s while CHECKION is waiting for sum-saliency.projects-a.plygrnd.tech to respond.

So the key points:
1. If they set SALIENCY_SERVICE_URL=https://sum-saliency.projects-a.plygrnd.tech, CHECKION will use that. Our route normalizes https to http for "internal" services - let me check. getSaliencyBaseUrl() converts https to http. So if they pass https://sum-saliency..., we'd convert it to http://sum-saliency... which might break if the public endpoint only serves HTTPS. I need to check - we force http to avoid SSL issues when calling internal services. If the user uses the public domain, the public domain might only listen on HTTPS, so we shouldn't force http in that case. Let me look at the code again.

getSaliencyBaseUrl(): if url starts with https, replace with http. So we'd turn https://sum-saliency.projects-a.plygrnd.tech into http://sum-saliency.projects-a.plygrnd.tech. If the public domain is only available via HTTPS, then http might not work (redirect or connection refused). So for a public domain we should NOT strip https. The comment says "internal services in Docker/Coolify typically do not use TLS" - so the intent was for internal URLs. For a public URL like https://sum-saliency.projects-a.plygrnd.tech we should keep https.

I'll change the logic to: only normalize to http if the URL looks like an internal host (e.g. no dot, or docker hostname), or we could simply not normalize when the user explicitly sets https. Easiest: only replace https with http when the hostname looks internal (e.g. doesn't contain a proper domain like .tech or .com). Or just keep https if it's already https - that way public domains work. So: if raw is https, keep it as https (don't force http). Then internal URLs would typically be http://container-id:8000 and work; public would be https://sum-saliency... and work. Let me re-read the original reason for forcing http - it was to fix "SSL packet length too long" when calling the saliency service. That might have been when calling an internal service that was exposed with HTTPS and had certificate issues. So the fix was to use http for internal. For an external/public URL with proper HTTPS, we should keep https. So I'll change to: only convert https to http if the hostname looks like an internal one (e.g. contains no TLD or is a single word). Simple heuristic: if hostname includes a dot and looks like a domain (e.g. .tech, .com), keep https; otherwise convert to http. Actually the simplest is: keep https as https. If the user has SSL issues with an internal URL they can set http. So just remove the https->http conversion, or make it conditional. I'll make it: if the URL is https and the hostname contains a dot (likely a real domain), keep https; else convert to http. So getSaliencyBaseUrl(): 
  if url starts with https and the host part has a dot (e.g. sum-saliency.projects-a.plygrnd.tech), return url as-is (https).
  else if url starts with https, return http version (internal).
  else return url.
</details></think>

Hier die Änderungen:

**1. Code (route.ts)**  
Die Saliency-URL wird nicht mehr pauschal auf `http` umgestellt.  
- **Öffentliche Domain** (Hostname enthält einen Punkt, z. B. `sum-saliency.projects-a.plygrnd.tech`): **HTTPS bleibt erhalten.**  
- **Interne Adresse** (z. B. Container-ID ohne Punkt): weiterhin Umstellung auf `http`, um SSL-Probleme zu vermeiden.

**2. Doku**  
In der Coolify-Anleitung steht jetzt, dass du entweder die interne URL (`http://<Container-ID>:8000`) oder die öffentliche Domain (z. B. `https://sum-saliency.projects-a.plygrnd.tech`) verwenden kannst.

**Was du einstellen musst**

In der **CHECKION**-App unter **Environment Variables**:

| Name | Wert |
|------|------|
| `SALIENCY_SERVICE_URL` | `https://sum-saliency.projects-a.plygrnd.tech` |

(Ohne abschließenden Slash. Wenn die SUM-App nur unter einer Subpath-URL erreichbar ist, z. B. `https://sum-saliency.projects-a.plygrnd.tech/saliency`, müsste der Code ggf. angepasst werden – üblich ist aber eine eigene Domain/Subdomain pro App.)

Danach CHECKION einmal **neu deployen**, damit die Variable geladen wird.

**Falls weiterhin 502 kommt:** Sehr oft ist das der **Proxy-Timeout** (z. B. Traefik 60 s). Die Saliency-Berechnung kann 1–3 Minuten dauern. In Coolify unter **Server → Proxy** die Timeouts für den HTTPS-Entrypoint erhöhen (z. B. `readTimeout=5m`). Details dazu stehen in der Doku unter „Fehler: 502 bei POST /api/saliency/generate“ (sobald der geplante Abschnitt eingepflegt ist).

_**Agent (default)**_


Die **502** kommt sehr wahrscheinlich vom **Proxy**: Nach **60 Sekunden** bricht Traefik die Verbindung ab, die Saliency-Berechnung braucht aber **1–3 Minuten**.

**Was du in Coolify ändern musst:**

1. **Server** öffnen (z. B. „projects-a“ / dein Server) → **Proxy** (oder **Proxy / Configuration**).
2. Prüfen, ob **Traefik** als Proxy genutzt wird.
3. In der Proxy-Konfiguration bei **Command** (bzw. **Argumente**) diese Timeouts **ergänzen**:
   ```yaml
   - '--entrypoints.https.transport.respondingTimeouts.readTimeout=5m'
   - '--entrypoints.https.transport.respondingTimeouts.writeTimeout=5m'
   - '--entrypoints.https.transport.respondingTimeouts.idleTimeout=5m'
   ```
4. **Speichern** und Proxy ggf. neu starten (Coolify macht das oft automatisch).
5. Nochmal **„Heatmap jetzt berechnen“** testen – die Anfrage kann dann bis zu 5 Minuten laufen.

Die Doku unter **„Fehler: 502 bei POST /api/saliency/generate“** ist entsprechend angepasst. Ohne diese Timeout-Erhöhung wird der Proxy die Verbindung weiter nach 60 s trennen und du bekommst 502, auch wenn `SALIENCY_SERVICE_URL` korrekt ist.

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The SUM saliency service is returning 500 with the error "name 'selective_scan_fn' is not defined". This is a runtime error in the SUM/mamba-ssm code - when we built with MAMBA_SKIP_CUDA_BUILD=TRUE, mamba-ssm was installed without the CUDA extension (selective_scan_cuda). So at runtime when the model tries to use the selective scan operation, it looks for selective_scan_fn which would normally be the CUDA kernel, but since we skipped the CUDA build, that function doesn't exist.

So the SUM model requires the mamba-ssm CUDA kernels to run - it doesn't have a pure PyTorch/CPU fallback for the selective scan. That means:
1. We can't run SUM on CPU-only with the current approach (skip CUDA build)
2. Options: (a) Run on a server with GPU and nvcc, build mamba-ssm and causal-conv1d with CUDA so selective_scan_fn is available; (b) Find if there's a CPU fallback in mamba-ssm we can enable; (c) Use the original MDS-ViTNet saliency service instead for CPU-only deployments

Let me search for selective_scan_fn in mamba-ssm and SUM to understand the flow.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

[Search code, repositories, users, issues, pull requests...](https://github.com/MzeroMiko/mamba-mini)\n4. [Search code, repositories, users, issues, pull requests...](https://github.com/state-spaces/mamba/issues/304)\n5. [selective_scan_interface.py](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/state-spaces/mamba/main/mamba_ssm/ops/selective_scan_interface.py)\n\n# Mamba-SSM Selective Scan CPU Fallback\n\nThe Mamba SSM library's &#96;selective_scan_fn&#96; requires CUDA for optimal performance, but there are CPU fallback implementations available:\n\n## Available CPU Solutions\n\n**mamba-cpu fork** - A dedicated CPU implementation of Mamba exists at proger/mamba-cpu[1], which is a fork of the official state-spaces/mamba repository designed to work without CUDA.\n\n**mamba-mini** - An efficient PyTorch implementation that works with both CPU and GPU in a single file[3]. This implementation is described as \"probably the code which is the most close to selective_scan_cuda in mamba\" and includes mathematical derivations.\n\n## Implementation Details\n\nThe official Mamba repository's &#96;selective_scan_interface.py&#96;[5] primarily uses &#96;selective_scan_cuda&#96; for forward and backward passes through the &#96;SelectiveScanFn&#96; autograd function. However, there is also a &#96;selective_scan_ref&#96; function that provides a reference implementation[5], which can serve as a pure Python/PyTorch fallback without CUDA dependencies.\n\nThe reference implementation handles the same inputs (u, delta, A, B, C, D, z, delta_bias, delta_softplus) but executes in standard PyTorch, making it suitable for CPU execution, though it will be slower than the optimized CUDA kernels.",

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So the official mamba has selective_scan_ref as a reference/CPU fallback. The issue is that when we install mamba-ssm with SKIP_CUDA_BUILD, the selective_scan_fn might not be set to the ref implementation - it might only be set when the CUDA extension is built. Let me look at the mamba-ssm selective_scan_interface.py to see how selective_scan_fn is assigned and if we can force the ref path.
</details></think>

```json
{
  "markdown": "# Copyright (c) 2023, Tri Dao, Albert Gu.\n\nimport torch\nimport torch.nn.functional as F\nfrom torch.cuda.amp import custom_bwd, custom_fwd\n\nfrom einops import rearrange, repeat\n\nfrom causal_conv1d import causal_conv1d_fn\nimport causal_conv1d_cuda\nimport selective_scan_cuda\n\n\nclass SelectiveScanFn(torch.autograd.Function):\n\n @staticmethod\n def forward(ctx, u, delta, A, B, C, D=None, z=None, delta_bias=None, delta_softplus=False,\n return_last_state=False):\n if u.stride(-1) != 1:\n u = u.contiguous()\n if delta.stride(-1) != 1:\n delta = delta.contiguous()\n if D is not None:\n D = D.contiguous()\n if B.stride(-1) != 1:\n B = B.contiguous()\n if C.stride(-1) != 1:\n C = C.contiguous()\n if z is not None and z.stride(-1) != 1:\n z = z.contiguous()\n if B.dim() == 3:\n B = rearrange(B, \"b dstate l -\u003e b 1 dstate l\")\n ctx.squeeze_B = True\n if C.dim() == 3:\n C = rearrange(C, \"b dstate l -\u003e b 1 dstate l\")\n ctx.squeeze_C = True\n out, x, *rest = selective_scan_cuda.fwd(u, delta, A, B, C, D, z, delta_bias, delta_softplus)\n ctx.delta_softplus = delta_softplus\n ctx.has_z = z is not None\n last_state = x[:, :, -1, 1::2] # (batch, dim, dstate)\n if not ctx.has_z:\n ctx.save_for_backward(u, delta, A, B, C, D, delta_bias, x)\n return out if not return_last_state else (out, last_state)\n else:\n ctx.save_for_backward(u, delta, A, B, C, D, z, delta_bias, x, out)\n out_z = rest[0]\n return out_z if not return_last_state else (out_z, last_state)\n\n @staticmethod\n def backward(ctx, dout, *args):\n if not ctx.has_z:\n u, delta, A, B, C, D, delta_bias, x = ctx.saved_tensors\n z = None\n out = None\n else:\n u, delta, A, B, C, D, z, delta_bias, x, out = ctx.saved_tensors\n if dout.stride(-1) != 1:\n dout = dout.contiguous()\n # The kernel supports passing in a pre-allocated dz (e.g., in case we want to fuse the\n # backward of selective_scan_cuda with the backward of chunk).\n # Here we just pass in None and dz will be allocated in the C++ code.\n du, ddelta, dA, dB, dC, dD, ddelta_bias, *rest = selective_scan_cuda.bwd(\n u, delta, A, B, C, D, z, delta_bias, dout, x, out, None, ctx.delta_softplus,\n False # option to recompute out_z, not used here\n )\n dz = rest[0] if ctx.has_z else None\n dB = dB.squeeze(1) if getattr(ctx, \"squeeze_B\", False) else dB\n dC = dC.squeeze(1) if getattr(ctx, \"squeeze_C\", False) else dC\n return (du, ddelta, dA, dB, dC,\n dD if D is not None else None,\n dz,\n ddelta_bias if delta_bias is not None else None,\n None,\n None)\n\n\ndef selective_scan_fn(u, delta, A, B, C, D=None, z=None, delta_bias=None, delta_softplus=False,\n return_last_state=False):\n \"\"\"if return_last_state is True, returns (out, last_state)\n last_state has shape (batch, dim, dstate). Note that the gradient of the last state is\n not considered in the backward pass.\n \"\"\"\n return SelectiveScanFn.apply(u, delta, A, B, C, D, z, delta_bias, delta_softplus, return_last_state)\n\n\ndef selective_scan_ref(u, delta, A, B, C, D=None, z=None, delta_bias=None, delta_softplus=False,\n return_last_state=False):\n \"\"\"\n u: r(B D L)\n delta: r(B D L)\n A: c(D N) or r(D N)\n B: c(D N) or r(B N L) or r(B N 2L) or r(B G N L) or (B G N L)\n C: c(D N) or r(B N L) or r(B N 2L) or r(B G N L) or (B G N L)\n D: r(D)\n z: r(B D L)\n delta_bias: r(D), fp32\n\n out: r(B D L)\n last_state (optional): r(B D dstate) or c(B D dstate)\n \"\"\"\n dtype_in = u.dtype\n u = u.float()\n delta = delta.float()\n if delta_bias is not None:\n delta = delta + delta_bias[..., None].float()\n if delta_softplus:\n delta = F.softplus(delta)\n batch, dim, dstate = u.shape[0], A.shape[0], A.shape[1]\n is_variable_B = B.dim() \u003e= 3\n is_variable_C = C.dim() \u003e= 3\n if A.is_complex():\n if is_variable_B:\n B = torch.view_as_complex(rearrange(B.float(), \"... (L two) -\u003e ... L two\", two=2))\n if is_variable_C:\n C = torch.view_as_complex(rearrange(C.float(), \"... (L two) -\u003e ... L two\", two=2))\n else:\n B = B.float()\n C = C.float()\n x = A.new_zeros((batch, dim, dstate))\n ys = []\n deltaA = torch.exp(torch.einsum('bdl,dn-\u003ebdln', delta, A))\n if not is_variable_B:\n deltaB_u = torch.einsum('bdl,dn,bdl-\u003ebdln', delta, B, u)\n else:\n if B.dim() == 3:\n deltaB_u = torch.einsum('bdl,bnl,bdl-\u003ebdln', delta, B, u)\n else:\n B = repeat(B, \"B G N L -\u003e B (G H) N L\", H=dim // B.shape[1])\n deltaB_u = torch.einsum('bdl,bdnl,bdl-\u003ebdln', delta, B, u)\n if is_variable_C and C.dim() == 4:\n C = repeat(C, \"B G N L -\u003e B (G H) N L\", H=dim // C.shape[1])\n last_state = None\n for i in range(u.shape[2]):\n x = deltaA[:, :, i] * x + deltaB_u[:, :, i]\n if not is_variable_C:\n y = torch.einsum('bdn,dn-\u003ebd', x, C)\n else:\n if C.dim() == 3:\n y = torch.einsum('bdn,bn-\u003ebd', x, C[:, :, i])\n else:\n y = torch.einsum('bdn,bdn-\u003ebd', x, C[:, :, :, i])\n if i == u.shape[2] - 1:\n last_state = x\n if y.is_complex():\n y = y.real * 2\n ys.append(y)\n y = torch.stack(ys, dim=2) # (batch dim L)\n out = y if D is None else y + u * rearrange(D, \"d -\u003e d 1\")\n if z is not None:\n out = out * F.silu(z)\n out = out.to(dtype=dtype_in)\n return out if not return_last_state else (out, last_state)\n\n\nclass MambaInnerFn(torch.autograd.Function):\n\n @staticmethod\n @custom_fwd\n def forward(ctx, xz, conv1d_weight, conv1d_bias, x_proj_weight, delta_proj_weight,\n out_proj_weight, out_proj_bias,\n A, B=None, C=None, D=None, delta_bias=None, B_proj_bias=None,\n C_proj_bias=None, delta_softplus=True, checkpoint_lvl=1):\n \"\"\"\n xz: (batch, dim, seqlen)\n \"\"\"\n assert checkpoint_lvl in [0, 1]\n L = xz.shape[-1]\n delta_rank = delta_proj_weight.shape[1]\n d_state = A.shape[-1] * (1 if not A.is_complex() else 2)\n if torch.is_autocast_enabled():\n x_proj_weight = x_proj_weight.to(dtype=torch.get_autocast_gpu_dtype())\n delta_proj_weight = delta_proj_weight.to(dtype=torch.get_autocast_gpu_dtype())\n out_proj_weight = out_proj_weight.to(dtype=torch.get_autocast_gpu_dtype())\n out_proj_bias = (out_proj_bias.to(dtype=torch.get_autocast_gpu_dtype())\n if out_proj_bias is not None else None)\n if xz.stride(-1) != 1:\n xz = xz.contiguous()\n conv1d_weight = rearrange(conv1d_weight, \"d 1 w -\u003e d w\")\n x, z = xz.chunk(2, dim=1)\n conv1d_bias = conv1d_bias.contiguous() if conv1d_bias is not None else None\n conv1d_out = causal_conv1d_cuda.causal_conv1d_fwd(x, conv1d_weight, conv1d_bias, True)\n # We're being very careful here about the layout, to avoid extra transposes.\n # We want delta to have d as the slowest moving dimension\n # and L as the fastest moving dimension, since those are what the ssm_scan kernel expects.\n x_dbl = F.linear(rearrange(conv1d_out, 'b d l -\u003e (b l) d'), x_proj_weight) # (bl d)\n delta = rearrange(delta_proj_weight @ x_dbl[:, :delta_rank].t(), \"d (b l) -\u003e b d l\", l = L)\n ctx.is_variable_B = B is None\n ctx.is_variable_C = C is None\n ctx.B_proj_bias_is_None = B_proj_bias is None\n ctx.C_proj_bias_is_None = C_proj_bias is None\n if B is None: # variable B\n B = x_dbl[:, delta_rank:delta_rank + d_state] # (bl dstate)\n if B_proj_bias is not None:\n B = B + B_proj_bias.to(dtype=B.dtype)\n if not A.is_complex():\n # B = rearrange(B, \"(b l) dstate -\u003e b dstate l\", l=L).contiguous()\n B = rearrange(B, \"(b l) dstate -\u003e b 1 dstate l\", l=L).contiguous()\n else:\n B = rearrange(B, \"(b l) (dstate two) -\u003e b 1 dstate (l two)\", l=L, two=2).contiguous()\n else:\n if B.stride(-1) != 1:\n B = B.contiguous()\n if C is None: # variable C\n C = x_dbl[:, -d_state:] # (bl dstate)\n if C_proj_bias is not None:\n C = C + C_proj_bias.to(dtype=C.dtype)\n if not A.is_complex():\n # C = rearrange(C, \"(b l) dstate -\u003e b dstate l\", l=L).contiguous()\n C = rearrange(C, \"(b l) dstate -\u003e b 1 dstate l\", l=L).contiguous()\n else:\n C = rearrange(C, \"(b l) (dstate two) -\u003e b 1 dstate (l two)\", l=L, two=2).contiguous()\n else:\n if C.stride(-1) != 1:\n C = C.contiguous()\n if D is not None:\n D = D.contiguous()\n out, scan_intermediates, out_z = selective_scan_cuda.fwd(\n conv1d_out, delta, A, B, C, D, z, delta_bias, delta_softplus\n )\n ctx.delta_softplus = delta_softplus\n ctx.out_proj_bias_is_None = out_proj_bias is None\n ctx.checkpoint_lvl = checkpoint_lvl\n if checkpoint_lvl \u003e= 1: # Will recompute conv1d_out and delta in the backward pass\n conv1d_out, delta = None, None\n ctx.save_for_backward(xz, conv1d_weight, conv1d_bias, x_dbl, x_proj_weight,\n delta_proj_weight, out_proj_weight, conv1d_out, delta,\n A, B, C, D, delta_bias, scan_intermediates, out)\n return F.linear(rearrange(out_z, \"b d l -\u003e b l d\"), out_proj_weight, out_proj_bias)\n\n @staticmethod\n @custom_bwd\n def backward(ctx, dout):\n # dout: (batch, seqlen, dim)\n (xz, conv1d_weight, conv1d_bias, x_dbl, x_proj_weight, delta_proj_weight, out_proj_weight,\n conv1d_out, delta, A, B, C, D, delta_bias, scan_intermediates, out) = ctx.saved_tensors\n L = xz.shape[-1]\n delta_rank = delta_proj_weight.shape[1]\n d_state = A.shape[-1] * (1 if not A.is_complex() else 2)\n x, z = xz.chunk(2, dim=1)\n if dout.stride(-1) != 1:\n dout = dout.contiguous()\n if ctx.checkpoint_lvl == 1:\n conv1d_out = causal_conv1d_cuda.causal_conv1d_fwd(x, conv1d_weight, conv1d_bias, True)\n delta = rearrange(delta_proj_weight @ x_dbl[:, :delta_rank].t(),\n \"d (b l) -\u003e b d l\", l = L)\n # The kernel supports passing in a pre-allocated dz (e.g., in case we want to fuse the\n # backward of selective_scan_cuda with the backward of chunk).\n dxz = torch.empty_like(xz) # (batch, dim, seqlen)\n dx, dz = dxz.chunk(2, dim=1)\n dout = rearrange(dout, \"b l e -\u003e e (b l)\")\n dout_y = rearrange(out_proj_weight.t() @ dout, \"d (b l) -\u003e b d l\", l=L)\n dconv1d_out, ddelta, dA, dB, dC, dD, ddelta_bias, dz, out_z = selective_scan_cuda.bwd(\n conv1d_out, delta, A, B, C, D, z, delta_bias, dout_y, scan_intermediates, out, dz,\n ctx.delta_softplus,\n True # option to recompute out_z\n )\n dout_proj_weight = torch.einsum(\"eB,dB-\u003eed\", dout, rearrange(out_z, \"b d l -\u003e d (b l)\"))\n dout_proj_bias = dout.sum(dim=(0, 1)) if not ctx.out_proj_bias_is_None else None\n dD = dD if D is not None else None\n dx_dbl = torch.empty_like(x_dbl)\n dB_proj_bias = None\n if ctx.is_variable_B:\n if not A.is_complex():\n dB = rearrange(dB, \"b 1 dstate l -\u003e (b l) dstate\").contiguous()\n else:\n dB = rearrange(dB, \"b 1 dstate (l two) -\u003e (b l) (dstate two)\", two=2).contiguous()\n dB_proj_bias = dB.sum(0) if not ctx.B_proj_bias_is_None else None\n dx_dbl[:, delta_rank:delta_rank + d_state] = dB # (bl d)\n dB = None\n dC_proj_bias = None\n if ctx.is_variable_C:\n if not A.is_complex():\n dC = rearrange(dC, \"b 1 dstate l -\u003e (b l) dstate\").contiguous()\n else:\n dC = rearrange(dC, \"b 1 dstate (l two) -\u003e (b l) (dstate two)\", two=2).contiguous()\n dC_proj_bias = dC.sum(0) if not ctx.C_proj_bias_is_None else None\n dx_dbl[:, -d_state:] = dC # (bl d)\n dC = None\n ddelta = rearrange(ddelta, \"b d l -\u003e d (b l)\")\n ddelta_proj_weight = torch.einsum(\"dB,Br-\u003edr\", ddelta, x_dbl[:, :delta_rank])\n dx_dbl[:, :delta_rank] = torch.einsum(\"dB,dr-\u003eBr\", ddelta, delta_proj_weight)\n dconv1d_out = rearrange(dconv1d_out, \"b d l -\u003e d (b l)\")\n dx_proj_weight = torch.einsum(\"Br,Bd-\u003erd\", dx_dbl, rearrange(conv1d_out, \"b d l -\u003e (b l) d\"))\n dconv1d_out = torch.addmm(dconv1d_out, x_proj_weight.t(), dx_dbl.t(), out=dconv1d_out)\n dconv1d_out = rearrange(dconv1d_out, \"d (b l) -\u003e b d l\", b=x.shape[0], l=x.shape[-1])\n # The kernel supports passing in a pre-allocated dx (e.g., in case we want to fuse the\n # backward of conv1d with the backward of chunk).\n dx, dconv1d_weight, dconv1d_bias = causal_conv1d_cuda.causal_conv1d_bwd(\n x, conv1d_weight, conv1d_bias, dconv1d_out, dx, True\n )\n dconv1d_bias = dconv1d_bias if conv1d_bias is not None else None\n dconv1d_weight = rearrange(dconv1d_weight, \"d w -\u003e d 1 w\")\n return (dxz, dconv1d_weight, dconv1d_bias, dx_proj_weight, ddelta_proj_weight,\n dout_proj_weight, dout_proj_bias,\n dA, dB, dC, dD,\n ddelta_bias if delta_bias is not None else None,\n dB_proj_bias, dC_proj_bias, None)\n\n\ndef mamba_inner_fn(\n xz, conv1d_weight, conv1d_bias, x_proj_weight, delta_proj_weight,\n out_proj_weight, out_proj_bias,\n A, B=None, C=None, D=None, delta_bias=None, B_proj_bias=None,\n C_proj_bias=None, delta_softplus=True\n):\n return MambaInnerFn.apply(xz, conv1d_weight, conv1d_bias, x_proj_weight, delta_proj_weight,\n out_proj_weight, out_proj_bias,\n A, B, C, D, delta_bias, B_proj_bias, C_proj_bias, delta_softplus)\n\n\ndef mamba_inner_ref(\n xz, conv1d_weight, conv1d_bias, x_proj_weight, delta_proj_weight,\n out_proj_weight, out_proj_bias,\n A, B=None, C=None, D=None, delta_bias=None, B_proj_bias=None,\n C_proj_bias=None, delta_softplus=True\n):\n L = xz.shape[-1]\n delta_rank = delta_proj_weight.shape[1]\n d_state = A.shape[-1] * (1 if not A.is_complex() else 2)\n x, z = xz.chunk(2, dim=1)\n x = causal_conv1d_fn(x, rearrange(conv1d_weight, \"d 1 w -\u003e d w\"), conv1d_bias, \"silu\")\n # We're being very careful here about the layout, to avoid extra transposes.\n # We want delta to have d as the slowest moving dimension\n # and L as the fastest moving dimension, since those are what the ssm_scan kernel expects.\n x_dbl = F.linear(rearrange(x, 'b d l -\u003e (b l) d'), x_proj_weight) # (bl d)\n delta = delta_proj_weight @ x_dbl[:, :delta_rank].t()\n delta = rearrange(delta, \"d (b l) -\u003e b d l\", l=L)\n if B is None: # variable B\n B = x_dbl[:, delta_rank:delta_rank + d_state] # (bl d)\n if B_proj_bias is not None:\n B = B + B_proj_bias.to(dtype=B.dtype)\n if not A.is_complex():\n B = rearrange(B, \"(b l) dstate -\u003e b dstate l\", l=L).contiguous()\n else:\n B = rearrange(B, \"(b l) (dstate two) -\u003e b dstate (l two)\", l=L, two=2).contiguous()\n if C is None: # variable B\n C = x_dbl[:, -d_state:] # (bl d)\n if C_proj_bias is not None:\n C = C + C_proj_bias.to(dtype=C.dtype)\n if not A.is_complex():\n C = rearrange(C, \"(b l) dstate -\u003e b dstate l\", l=L).contiguous()\n else:\n C = rearrange(C, \"(b l) (dstate two) -\u003e b dstate (l two)\", l=L, two=2).contiguous()\n y = selective_scan_fn(x, delta, A, B, C, D, z=z, delta_bias=delta_bias, delta_softplus=True)\n return F.linear(rearrange(y, \"b d l -\u003e b l d\"), out_proj_weight, out_proj_bias)\n",
  "url": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/state-spaces/mamba/v1.0.1/mamba_ssm/ops/selective_scan_interface.py"
}
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I see. The mamba_ssm 1.0.1 code:
1. Imports `selective_scan_cuda` and `causal_conv1d_cuda` at module level - when we skip CUDA build, these modules don't exist, so the import would fail at startup. But the user said the service started and returned health - so maybe the import is lazy or the error happens only when /predict is called and the model runs.
2. Actually re-reading the error: "name 'selective_scan_fn' is not defined" - that's a NameError, meaning the name isn't defined. In the file, selective_scan_fn is defined at the bottom. So the issue might be that when we have SKIP_CUDA_BUILD, the module fails to import (import selective_scan_cuda fails) and the module loading might be caught somewhere, or a different code path is used. Let me check - if import selective_scan_cuda fails, the whole selective_scan_interface.py would fail to load, so selective_scan_fn would never be defined. So somewhere in SUM or mamba the code might try to use selective_scan_fn when the CUDA module failed to load - or there's a different version of the file that conditionally defines selective_scan_fn.

Actually re-reading again: the error says "name 'selective_scan_fn' is not defined". So the code that's running is trying to reference selective_scan_fn but it's not defined. That could happen if:
1. selective_scan_interface.py failed to load (due to import selective_scan_cuda failing), so a different stub or fallback module is used that doesn't define selective_scan_fn.
2. Or there's a try/except that catches the import and then later code expects selective_scan_fn to exist.

Let me check the main mamba_ssm __init__ or where selective_scan_fn is used - maybe they have a fallback that uses selective_scan_ref when cuda is not available. I'll search for where selective_scan_fn is set or imported in mamba.
</details></think>

[Search code, repositories, users, issues, pull requests...](https://github.com/state-spaces/mamba/issues/652)\n\n# Mamba-SSM 1.0.1 Import Error Solutions\n\nWhen &#96;selective_scan_cuda&#96; fails to import in mamba-ssm 1.0.1, you can use the reference implementation as a fallback. Here's what to know:\n\n## The Problem\nThe error occurs when importing &#96;selective_scan_cuda&#96; due to undefined symbols in the compiled CUDA extension.[1][3] This typically happens when there's a mismatch between your PyTorch, CUDA, and the pre-compiled wheel.[4]\n\n## Solutions\n\n**1. Use the Reference Implementation**\nThe code includes a fallback to &#96;selective_scan_ref&#96; (the reference implementation) when the CUDA version fails to import. The selective scan interface in &#96;mamba_ssm/ops/selective_scan_interface.py&#96; should handle this gracefully, allowing you to use the slower CPU/reference version instead of the optimized CUDA kernel.\n\n**2. Install the Correct Wheel**\nThe most reliable fix is to download the correct pre-built wheel for your environment from the [GitHub releases page](https://github.com/state-spaces/mamba/releases). Ensure the wheel matches your:[4]\n- CUDA version (e.g., cu118, cu12)\n- PyTorch version (e.g., torch2.3)\n- Python version (e.g., cp310 for Python 3.10)\n- CXX11 ABI compatibility\n\n**3. Build from Source**\nIf wheels don't work, try building from source with &#96;pip install . --no-build-isolation&#96; to ensure compatibility with your environment.[2]\n\nThe reference implementation will work but will be significantly slower than the optimized CUDA version.",

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So the mamba_ssm 1.0.1 selective_scan_interface.py that I saw does NOT have a try/except fallback - it directly imports selective_scan_cuda. So when we install with SKIP_CUDA_BUILD, the selective_scan_cuda module doesn't exist. The import would fail when anything imports from mamba_ssm.ops.selective_scan_interface. So how does the SUM container even start? Maybe the SUM code or mamba imports this lazily - e.g. only when the model runs. So at startup (health check) nothing imports selective_scan_cuda, but when /predict is called and the model runs, it tries to use selective_scan_fn and at that point the module that defines it wasn't fully loaded because the import of selective_scan_cuda failed somewhere up the chain. So we need to patch the mamba-ssm package to use selective_scan_ref when selective_scan_cuda is not available. That would require either: 1. Installing a patched version of mamba-ssm that has try/except import and uses selective_scan_ref when cuda fails 2. Or monkey-patching at runtime in main_sum.py before importing SUM Let me check the state-spaces/mamba repo for a newer version that might have CPU fallback.
</details></think>

(batch, dim, dstate)\n if not ctx.has_z:\n ctx.save_for_backward(u, delta, A, B, C, D, delta_bias, x)\n return out if not return_last_state else (out, last_state)\n else:\n ctx.save_for_backward(u, delta, A, B, C, D, z, delta_bias, x, out)\n out_z = rest[0]\n return out_z if not return_last_state else (out_z, last_state)\n\n @staticmethod\n def backward(ctx, dout, *args):\n if not ctx.has_z:\n u, delta, A, B, C, D, delta_bias, x = ctx.saved_tensors\n z = None\n out = None\n else:\n u, delta, A, B, C, D, z, delta_bias, x, out = ctx.saved_tensors\n if dout.stride(-1) != 1:\n dout = dout.contiguous()\n # option to recompute out_z, not used here\n )\n dz = rest[0] if ctx.has_z else None\n dB = dB.squeeze(1) if getattr(ctx, \"squeeze_B\", False) else dB\n dC = dC.squeeze(1) if getattr(ctx, \"squeeze_C\", False) else dC\n return (du, ddelta, dA, dB, dC,\n dD if D is not None else None,\n dz,\n ddelta_bias if delta_bias is not None else None,\n None,\n None)\n\n\ndef rms_norm_forward(\n x,\n weight,\n bias,\n eps=1e-6,\n is_rms_norm=True,\n):\n # x (b l) d\n if x.stride(-1) != 1:\n x = x.contiguous()\n weight = weight.contiguous()\n if bias is not None:\n bias = bias.contiguous()\n y = _layer_norm_fwd(\n x, weight, bias, eps, None, residual_dtype=None, is_rms_norm=is_rms_norm\n )[0]\n # y (b l) d\n return y\n\n\ndef selective_scan_fn(u, delta, A, B, C, D=None, z=None, delta_bias=None, delta_softplus=False,\n return_last_state=False):\n \"\"\"if return_last_state is True, returns (out, last_state)\n last_state has shape (batch, dim, dstate). Note that the gradient of the last state is\n not considered in the backward pass.\n \"\"\"\n return SelectiveScanFn.apply(u, delta, A, B, C, D, z, delta_bias, delta_softplus, return_last_state)\n\n\ndef selective_scan_ref(u, delta, A, B, C, D=None, z=None, delta_bias=None, delta_softplus=False,\n return_last_state=False):\n \"\"\"\n u: r(B D L)\n delta: r(B D L)\n A: c(D N) or r(D N)\n B: c(D N) or r(B N L) or r(B N 2L) or r(B G N L) or (B G N L)\n C: c(D N) or r(B N L) or r(B N 2L) or r(B G N L) or (B G N L)\n D: r(D)\n z: r(B D L)\n delta_bias: r(D), fp32\n\n out: r(B D L)\n last_state (optional): r(B D dstate) or c(B D dstate)\n \"\"\"\n dtype_in = u.dtype\n u = u.float()\n delta = delta.float()\n if delta_bias is not None:\n delta = delta + delta_bias[..., None].float()\n if delta_softplus:\n delta = F.softplus(delta)\n batch, dim, dstate = u.shape[0], A.shape[0], A.shape[1]\n is_variable_B = B.dim() \u003e= 3\n is_variable_C = C.dim() \u003e= 3\n if A.is_complex():\n if is_variable_B:\n B = torch.view_as_complex(rearrange(B.float(), \"... (L two) -\u003e ... L two\", two=2))\n if is_variable_C:\n C = torch.view_as_complex(rearrange(C.float(), \"... (L two) -\u003e ... L two\", two=2))\n else:\n B = B.float()\n C = C.float()\n x = A.new_zeros((batch, dim, dstate))\n ys = []\n deltaA = torch.exp(torch.einsum('bdl,dn-\u003ebdln', delta, A))\n if not is_variable_B:\n deltaB_u = torch.einsum('bdl,dn,bdl-\u003ebdln', delta, B, u)\n else:\n if B.dim() == 3:\n deltaB_u = torch.einsum('bdl,bnl,bdl-\u003ebdln', delta, B, u)\n else:\n B = repeat(B, \"B G N L -\u003e B (G H) N L\", H=dim // B.shape[1])\n deltaB_u = torch.einsum('bdl,bdnl,bdl-\u003ebdln', delta, B, u)\n if is_variable_C and C.dim() == 4:\n C = repeat(C, \"B G N L -\u003e B (G H) N L\", H=dim // C.shape[1])\n last_state = None\n for i in range(u.shape[2]):\n x = deltaA[:, :, i] * x + deltaB_u[:, :, i]\n if not is_variable_C:\n y = torch.einsum('bdn,dn-\u003ebd', x, C)\n else:\n if C.dim() == 3:\n y = torch.einsum('bdn,bn-\u003ebd', x, C[:, :, i])\n else:\n y = torch.einsum('bdn,bdn-\u003ebd', x, C[:, :, :, i])\n if i == u.shape[2] - 1:\n last_state = x\n if y.is_complex():\n y = y.real * 2\n ys.append(y)\n y = torch.stack(ys, dim=2) # (batch dim L)\n out = y if D is None else y + u * rearrange(D, \"d -\u003e d 1\")\n if z is not None:\n out = out * F.silu(z)\n out = out.to(dtype=dtype_in)\n return out if not return_last_state else (out, last_state)\n\n\nclass MambaInnerFn(torch.autograd.Function):\n\n @staticmethod\n @custom_fwd\n def forward(ctx, xz, conv1d_weight, conv1d_bias, x_proj_weight, delta_proj_weight,\n out_proj_weight, out_proj_bias,\n A, B=None, C=None, D=None, delta_bias=None, B_proj_bias=None,\n C_proj_bias=None, delta_softplus=True, checkpoint_lvl=1, b_rms_weight=None, c_rms_weight= None, dt_rms_weight= None, b_c_dt_rms_eps=1e-6):\n \"\"\"\n xz: (batch, dim, seqlen)\n \"\"\"\n assert causal_conv1d_fwd_function is not None, \"causal_conv1d_cuda is not available. Please install causal-conv1d.\"\n assert checkpoint_lvl in [0, 1]\n L = xz.shape[-1]\n delta_rank = delta_proj_weight.shape[1]\n d_state = A.shape[-1] * (1 if not A.is_complex() else 2)\n if torch.is_autocast_enabled():\n x_proj_weight = x_proj_weight.to(dtype=torch.get_autocast_gpu_dtype())\n delta_proj_weight = delta_proj_weight.to(dtype=torch.get_autocast_gpu_dtype())\n out_proj_weight = out_proj_weight.to(dtype=torch.get_autocast_gpu_dtype())\n out_proj_bias = (out_proj_bias.to(dtype=torch.get_autocast_gpu_dtype())\n if out_proj_bias is not None else None)\n if xz.stride(-1) != 1:\n xz = xz.contiguous()\n conv1d_weight = rearrange(conv1d_weight, \"d 1 w -\u003e d w\")\n x, z = xz.chunk(2, dim=1)\n conv1d_bias = conv1d_bias.contiguous() if conv1d_bias is not None else None\n conv1d_out = causal_conv1d_fwd_function(\n x, conv1d_weight, conv1d_bias, None, None, None, True\n )\n # We're being very careful here about the layout, to avoid extra transposes.\n # We want delta to have d as the slowest moving dimension\n # and L as the fastest moving dimension, since those are what the ssm_scan kernel expects.\n x_dbl = F.linear(rearrange(conv1d_out, 'b d l -\u003e (b l) d'), x_proj_weight) # (bl d)\n delta = rearrange(delta_proj_weight @ x_dbl[:, :delta_rank].t(), \"d (b l) -\u003e b d l\", l = L)\n ctx.is_variable_B = B is None\n ctx.is_variable_C = C is None\n ctx.B_proj_bias_is_None = B_proj_bias is None\n ctx.C_proj_bias_is_None = C_proj_bias is None\n if B is None: # variable B\n B = x_dbl[:, delta_rank:delta_rank + d_state] # (bl dstate)\n if B_proj_bias is not None:\n B = B + B_proj_bias.to(dtype=B.dtype)\n if not A.is_complex():\n # B = rearrange(B, \"(b l) dstate -\u003e b dstate l\", l=L).contiguous()\n B = rearrange(B, \"(b l) dstate -\u003e b 1 dstate l\", l=L).contiguous()\n else:\n B = rearrange(B, \"(b l) (dstate two) -\u003e b 1 dstate (l two)\", l=L, two=2).contiguous()\n else:\n if B.stride(-1) != 1:\n B = B.contiguous()\n if C is None: # variable C\n C = x_dbl[:, -d_state:] # (bl dstate)\n if C_proj_bias is not None:\n C = C + C_proj_bias.to(dtype=C.dtype)\n if not A.is_complex():\n # C = rearrange(C, \"(b l) dstate -\u003e b dstate l\", l=L).contiguous()\n C = rearrange(C, \"(b l) dstate -\u003e b 1 dstate l\", l=L).contiguous()\n else:\n C = rearrange(C, \"(b l) (dstate two) -\u003e b 1 dstate (l two)\", l=L, two=2).contiguous()\n else:\n if C.stride(-1) != 1:\n C = C.contiguous()\n if D is not None:\n D = D.contiguous()\n \n if b_rms_weight is not None:\n B = rearrange(B, \"b 1 dstate l -\u003e (b l) dstate\", l=L).contiguous()\n B = rms_norm_forward(B, b_rms_weight, bias=None, eps=b_c_dt_rms_eps)\n B = rearrange(B, \"(b l) dstate -\u003e b 1 dstate l\", l=L).contiguous()\n if c_rms_weight is not None:\n C = rearrange(C, \"b 1 dstate l -\u003e (b l) dstate\", l=L).contiguous()\n C = rms_norm_forward(C, c_rms_weight, bias=None, eps=b_c_dt_rms_eps)\n C = rearrange(C, \"(b l) dstate -\u003e b 1 dstate l\", l=L).contiguous()\n if dt_rms_weight is not None:\n delta = rearrange(delta, \"b d l -\u003e (b l) d\", l=L).contiguous()\n delta = rms_norm_forward(delta, dt_rms_weight, bias=None, eps=b_c_dt_rms_eps)\n delta = rearrange(delta, \"(b l) d -\u003e b d l\", l=L).contiguous()\n \n out, scan_intermediates, out_z = selective_scan_cuda.fwd(\n conv1d_out, delta, A, B, C, D, z, delta_bias, delta_softplus\n )\n ctx.delta_softplus = delta_softplus\n ctx.out_proj_bias_is_None = out_proj_bias is None\n ctx.checkpoint_lvl = checkpoint_lvl\n ctx.b_rms_weight = b_rms_weight\n ctx.c_rms_weight = c_rms_weight\n ctx.dt_rms_weight = dt_rms_weight\n ctx.b_c_dt_rms_eps = b_c_dt_rms_eps\n if checkpoint_lvl \u003e= 1: # Will recompute conv1d_out and delta in the backward pass\n conv1d_out, delta = None, None\n ctx.save_for_backward(xz, conv1d_weight, conv1d_bias, x_dbl, x_proj_weight,\n delta_proj_weight, out_proj_weight, conv1d_out, delta,\n A, B, C, D, delta_bias, scan_intermediates, b_rms_weight, c_rms_weight, dt_rms_weight, out)\n return F.linear(rearrange(out_z, \"b d l -\u003e b l d\"), out_proj_weight, out_proj_bias)\n\n @staticmethod\n @custom_bwd\n def backward(ctx, dout):\n # dout: (batch, seqlen, dim)\n assert causal_conv1d_fwd_function is not None, \"causal_conv1d_cuda is not available. Please install causal-conv1d.\"\n (xz, conv1d_weight, conv1d_bias, x_dbl, x_proj_weight, delta_proj_weight, out_proj_weight,\n conv1d_out, delta, A, B, C, D, delta_bias, scan_intermediates, b_rms_weight, c_rms_weight, dt_rms_weight, out) = ctx.saved_tensors\n L = xz.shape[-1]\n delta_rank = delta_proj_weight.shape[1]\n d_state = A.shape[-1] * (1 if not A.is_complex() else 2)\n x, z = xz.chunk(2, dim=1)\n if dout.stride(-1) != 1:\n dout = dout.contiguous()\n if ctx.checkpoint_lvl == 1:\n conv1d_out = causal_conv1d_fwd_function(\n x, conv1d_weight, conv1d_bias, None, None, None, True\n )\n delta = rearrange(delta_proj_weight @ x_dbl[:, :delta_rank].t(),\n \"d (b l) -\u003e b d l\", l = L)\n if dt_rms_weight is not None:\n delta = rearrange(delta, \"b d l -\u003e (b l) d\", l=L).contiguous()\n delta = rms_norm_forward(delta, ctx.dt_rms_weight, None, ctx.b_c_dt_rms_eps)\n delta = rearrange(delta, \"(b l) d -\u003e b d l\", l=L).contiguous()\n if b_rms_weight is not None:\n # option to recompute out_z\n )\n dout_proj_weight = torch.einsum(\"eB,dB-\u003eed\", dout, rearrange(out_z, \"b d l -\u003e d (b l)\"))\n dout_proj_bias = dout.sum(dim=(0, 1)) if not ctx.out_proj_bias_is_None else None\n dD = dD if D is not None else None\n dx_dbl = torch.empty_like(x_dbl)\n dB_proj_bias = None\n if ctx.is_variable_B:\n if not A.is_complex():\n dB = rearrange(dB, \"b 1 dstate l -\u003e (b l) dstate\").contiguous()\n else:\n dB = rearrange(dB, \"b 1 dstate (l two) -\u003e (b l) (dstate two)\", two=2).contiguous()\n dB_proj_bias = dB.sum(0) if not ctx.B_proj_bias_is_None else None\n dx_dbl[:, delta_rank:delta_rank + d_state] = dB # The kernel supports passing in a pre-allocated dx (e.g., in case we want to fuse the\n # backward of conv1d with the backward of chunk).\n dx, dconv1d_weight, dconv1d_bias, *_ = causal_conv1d_bwd_function(\n x, conv1d_weight, conv1d_bias, dconv1d_out, None, None, None, dx, False, True\n )\n dconv1d_bias = dconv1d_bias if conv1d_bias is not None else None\n dconv1d_weight = rearrange(dconv1d_weight, \"d w -\u003e d 1 w\")\n return (dxz, dconv1d_weight, dconv1d_bias, dx_proj_weight, ddelta_proj_weight,\n dout_proj_weight, dout_proj_bias,\n dA, dB, dC, dD,\n ddelta_bias if delta_bias is not None else None,\n # 6-None are delta_softplus, checkpoint_lvl, b_rms_weight, c_rms_weight, dt_rms_weight, b_c_dt_rms_eps\n dB_proj_bias, dC_proj_bias, None, None, None, None, None, None)\n\n\ndef mamba_inner_fn(\n xz, conv1d_weight, conv1d_bias, x_proj_weight, delta_proj_weight,\n out_proj_weight, out_proj_bias,\n A, B=None, C=None, D=None, delta_bias=None, B_proj_bias=None,\n C_proj_bias=None, delta_softplus=True, checkpoint_lvl=1, b_rms_weight= None, c_rms_weight= None, dt_rms_weight= None, b_c_dt_rms_eps=1e-6\n):\n return MambaInnerFn.apply(xz, conv1d_weight, conv1d_bias, x_proj_weight, delta_proj_weight,\n out_proj_weight, out_proj_bias,\n A, B, C, D, delta_bias, B_proj_bias, C_proj_bias, delta_softplus, checkpoint_lvl, b_rms_weight, c_rms_weight, dt_rms_weight, b_c_dt_rms_eps)\n\n\ndef mamba_inner_ref(\n xz, conv1d_weight, conv1d_bias, x_proj_weight, delta_proj_weight,\n out_proj_weight, out_proj_bias,\n A, B=None, C=None, D=None, delta_bias=None, B_proj_bias=None,\n C_proj_bias=None, delta_softplus=True\n):\n assert causal_conv1d_fn is not None, \"causal_conv1d_fn is not available. Please install causal-conv1d.\"\n L = xz.shape[-1]\n delta_rank = delta_proj_weight.shape[1]\n d_state = A.shape[-1] * (1 if not A.is_complex() else 2)\n x, z = xz.chunk(2, dim=1)\n x = causal_conv1d_fn(x, rearrange(conv1d_weight, \"d 1 w -\u003e d w\"), conv1d_bias, activation=\"silu\")\n # We're being very careful here about the layout, to avoid extra transposes.\n # We want delta to have d as the slowest moving dimension\n # and L as the fastest moving dimension, since those are what the ssm_scan kernel expects.\n x_dbl = F.linear(rearrange(x, 'b d l -\u003e (b l) d'), x_proj_weight) # (bl d)\n delta = delta_proj_weight @ x_dbl[:, :delta_rank].t()\n delta = rearrange(delta, \"d (b l) -\u003e b d l\", l=L)\n if B is None: # variable B\n B = x_dbl[:, delta_rank:delta_rank + d_state] # (bl d)\n if B_proj_bias is not None:\n B = B + B_proj_bias.to(dtype=B.dtype)\n if not A.is_complex():\n B = rearrange(B, \"(b l) dstate -\u003e b dstate l\", l=L).contiguous()\n else:\n B = rearrange(B, \"(b l) (dstate two) -\u003e b dstate (l two)\", l=L, two=2).contiguous()\n if C is None: # variable B\n C = x_dbl[:, -d_state:] # (bl d)\n if C_proj_bias is not None:\n C = C + C_proj_bias.to(dtype=C.dtype)\n if not A.is_complex():\n C = rearrange(C, \"(b l) dstate -\u003e b dstate l\", l=L).contiguous()\n else:\n C = rearrange(C, \"(b l) (dstate two) -\u003e b dstate (l two)\", l=L, two=2).contiguous()\n y = selective_scan_fn(x, delta, A, B, C, D, z=z, delta_bias=delta_bias, delta_softplus=True)\n return F.linear(rearrange(y, \"b d l -\u003e b l d\"), out_proj_weight, out_proj_bias)\n",
```json
  "url": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/state-spaces/mamba/main/mamba_ssm/ops/selective_scan_interface.py"
}
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So the issue is:
1. SUM uses mamba-ssm 1.0.1 (pinned in SUM requirements)
2. mamba-ssm 1.0.1's selective_scan_interface.py does `import selective_scan_cuda` at top level - no try/except. When we built with SKIP_CUDA_BUILD, the selective_scan_cuda module wasn't built, so it doesn't exist.
3. So when would the error "selective_scan_fn is not defined" happen? It would happen if the module partially loads - e.g. maybe selective_scan_interface is imported from somewhere that catches the import error and then later code tries to use selective_scan_fn. Let me check where the error might come from. The error says "name 'selective_scan_fn' is not defined" - that could be in mamba_inner_ref which calls selective_scan_fn(x, delta, A, B, C, D, ...). So maybe there's a code path that uses mamba_inner_ref when CUDA is not available, and mamba_inner_ref calls selective_scan_fn - but if the module failed to load due to import selective_scan_cuda, then selective_scan_fn was never defined. So the flow might be: some other module (e.g. mamba_ssm/__init__.py or a block) does try: from .ops.selective_scan_interface import selective_scan_fn except: selective_scan_fn = None or use ref. Then later the model code uses mamba_inner_ref which internally calls selective_scan_fn - but that's the ref implementation's selective_scan_fn. So if the interface module failed to load, selective_scan_fn isn't defined anywhere. So we need to patch mamba_ssm so that when selective_scan_cuda is not available, we use selective_scan_ref as selective_scan_fn. The cleanest would be to add a small Python file in our saliency-service that we install or we inject before importing SUM - that monkey-patches mamba_ssm to use the ref implementation when cuda isn't available. Let me check how SUM imports mamba - it's through the SUM repo's net module. So the flow is: main_sum.py adds SUM to path, then "from net import SUM, ...". The SUM net code will import mamba_ssm. So we need to patch mamba_ssm before we do "from net import SUM". We can in main_sum.py: 1. sys.path.insert(0, str(SUM_ROOT)) 2. Patch mamba_ssm: try to import mamba_ssm.ops.selective_scan_interface - if it fails (ImportError), create a stub module that provides selective_scan_fn = selective_scan_ref and mamba_inner_fn = mamba_inner_ref, and inject it. But the ref implementation is inside the same file that failed to load. So we need a different approach: we need to patch the selective_scan_interface.py file in the installed mamba_ssm package so that it uses try/except around import selective_scan_cuda and if it fails, defines selective_scan_fn = selective_scan_ref. And MambaInnerFn would need to use the ref path too when cuda is not available - but MambaInnerFn directly uses selective_scan_cuda and causal_conv1d_cuda. So when CUDA is not available, the whole MambaInnerFn can't work - we'd need mamba_inner_ref. So the model (SUM) probably checks somewhere whether to use mamba_inner_fn or mamba_inner_ref. Let me search the SUM repo and mamba for where they choose between the two.
</details></think>

[Search code, repositories, users, issues, pull requests...](https://github.com/state-spaces/mamba/issues/304)\n\n# Mamba Selective Scan Reference Implementation\n\nThe Mamba library includes a &#96;selective_scan_ref&#96; function as a reference implementation of the selective scan operation.[2] This reference function is used as a fallback when the optimized CUDA version is not available.\n\n## Key Implementation Details\n\nThe &#96;selective_scan_ref&#96; function is defined in &#96;selective_scan_interface.py&#96; and accepts the same parameters as the CUDA-optimized version:[2]\n\n- **Inputs**: u, delta, A, B, C, D (optional), z (optional), delta_bias (optional), delta_softplus flag\n- **Outputs**: out (and optionally last_state)\n\nThe reference implementation converts input tensors to float precision for computation and handles various tensor shapes and configurations.[2]\n\n## CUDA Fallback Mechanism\n\nThe library uses &#96;selective_scan_cuda.fwd()&#96; and &#96;selective_scan_cuda.bwd()&#96; for the optimized forward and backward passes when CUDA is available.[2] The &#96;SelectiveScanFn&#96; class implements the autograd Function that calls these CUDA kernels.\n\nWhen CUDA operations are not available or the user needs a pure PyTorch reference implementation, the &#96;selective_scan_ref&#96; function provides a differentiable alternative that can be used instead of the CUDA-accelerated version.",
