```json
    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "detailedLines": [
          {
            "isSignature": true,
            "lineNumber": 103,
            "text": "export default function ResultsPage() {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 975
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 976,
            "text": "                                    onLoad={(e) =\u003e {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 977,
            "text": "                                        const el = e.currentTarget;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 978,
            "text": "                                        setScreenshotDimensions({ width: el.naturalWidth, height: el.naturalHeight });"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 979,
            "text": "                                    }}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 980,
            "text": "                                /\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 981,
            "text": "                                {/* Overlay layer: same size as image (100% × 100%) so positioning scales with screenshot */}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 982,
            "text": "                                \u003cBox sx={{ position: 'absolute', top: 0, left: 0, width: '100%', height: '100%', pointerEvents: 'none', zIndex: 10 }}\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 983,
            "text": "                                    {result.ux?.focusOrder \u0026\u0026 ("
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 984,
            "text": "                                        \u003cBox sx={{ position: 'absolute', top: 0, left: 0, width: '100%', height: '100%', zIndex: 40 }}\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 985,
            "text": "                                            \u003cFocusOrderOverlay"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 986,
            "text": "                                                items={result.ux.focusOrder}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 987,
            "text": "                                                screenshotWidth={screenshotDimensions.width}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 988,
            "text": "                                                screenshotHeight={screenshotDimensions.height}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 989,
            "text": "                                                visible={showFocusOrder}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 990,
            "text": "                                            /\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 991,
            "text": "                                        \u003c/Box\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 992,
            "text": "                                    )}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 993,
            "text": "                                    {result.ux?.tapTargets?.details \u0026\u0026 ("
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 994,
            "text": "                                        \u003cBox sx={{ position: 'absolute', top: 0, left: 0, width: '100%', height: '100%', zIndex: 45 }}\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 995,
            "text": "                                            {showTouchTargets \u0026\u0026 ("
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 996,
            "text": "                                                \u003cTouchTargetOverlay"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 997,
            "text": "                                                    issues={result.ux.tapTargets.details}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 998,
            "text": "                                                    screenshotWidth={screenshotDimensions.width}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 999,

            "text": "export default function ResultsPage() {"

          {
            "lineNumber": 1024,
            "text": "(issue, idx) =\u003e ("
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1033
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1034,
            "text": "                                                        \u003c/Box\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1035,
            "text": "                                                        \u003cMsqdxTypography variant=\"caption\" sx={{ display: 'block', mt: 0.5 }}\u003e{issue.selector}\u003c/MsqdxTypography\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1036,
            "text": "                                                    \u003c/Box\u003e"

            "text": "                                                }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1038,
            "text": "                                                brandColor={SEVERITY_CONFIG[issue.type].label === 'Error' ? 'pink' : SEVERITY_CONFIG[issue.type].label === 'Warning' ? 'yellow' : 'purple'}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1039,
            "text": "                                            \u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1040,
            "text": "                                                \u003cBox"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1041,
            "text": "                                                    sx={{"
          },

            "lineNumber": 1042,
            "text": "                                                        position: 'absolute',"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1043,
            "text": "                                                        left: &#96;${(issue.boundingBox.x / screenshotDimensions.width) * 100}%&#96;,"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1044,
            "text": "                                                        top: &#96;${(issue.boundingBox.y / screenshotDimensions.height) * 100}%&#96;,"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1045,
            "text": "                                                        width: &#96;${(issue.boundingBox.width / screenshotDimensions.width) * 100}%&#96;,"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1046,
            "text": "                                                        height: &#96;${(issue.boundingBox.height / screenshotDimensions.height) * 100}%&#96;,"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1047,
            "text": "                                                        border: highlightedIndex === idx ? &#96;4px solid ${SEVERITY_CONFIG[issue.type].color}&#96; : &#96;3px solid ${SEVERITY_CONFIG[issue.type].color}&#96;,"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1048,
            "text": "                                                        backgroundColor: highlightedIndex === idx ? alpha(SEVERITY_CONFIG[issue.type].color, 0.5) : alpha(SEVERITY_CONFIG[issue.type].color, 0.2),"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1049,
            "text": "                                                        cursor: 'pointer',"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1050,
            "text": "                                                        zIndex: highlightedIndex === idx ? 30 : 10,"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1051,
            "text": "                                                        transition: 'all 0.2s',"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1052,
            "text": "                                                        boxShadow: highlightedIndex === idx ? &#96;0 0 0 4px ${alpha(SEVERITY_CONFIG[issue.type].color, 0.4)}&#96; : 'none',"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1053,
            "text": "                                                        pointerEvents: 'auto',"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1054,
            "text": "                                                        "

    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "detailedLines": [
          {
            "isSignature": true,
            "lineNumber": 103,
            "text": "export default function ResultsPage() {"
          },

            "lineNumber": 1017
          },

            "lineNumber": 1018,
            "text": "                                            screenshotHeight={screenshotDimensions.height}"

            "lineNumber": 1019,

          },
          {

          },

          },

            "text": "                                    )}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1023,
            "text": "                                    {/* Issue boxes: percentage-based so they stay aligned when screenshot is scaled */}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1024,
            "text": "                                    {filteredIssues.map((issue, idx) =\u003e ("
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1025,
            "text": "                                        issue.boundingBox \u0026\u0026 ("
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1026,
            "text": "                                            \u003cMsqdxTooltip"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1027,
            "text": "                                                key={idx}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1028,

          },

            "text": "                                                    \u003cBox\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1030,
            "text": "                                                        \u003cMsqdxTypography variant=\"subtitle2\" sx={{ fontWeight: 'bold' }}\u003e{issue.message}\u003c/MsqdxTypography\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1031,
            "text": "                                                        \u003cBox sx={{ display: 'flex', gap: 1, mt: 0.5 }}\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1032,
            "text": "                                                            \u003cMsqdxChip label={issue.type} size=\"small\" sx={{ height: 16, fontSize: '0.6rem', bgcolor: SEVERITY_CONFIG[issue.type].color, color: '#000' }} /\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1033,
            "text": "                                                            {issue.wcagLevel \u0026\u0026 issue.wcagLevel !== 'Unknown' \u0026\u0026 \u003cMsqdxChip label={issue.wcagLevel === 'APCA' ? 'APCA' : &#96;Level ${issue.wcagLevel}&#96;} size=\"small\" sx={{ height: 16, fontSize: '0.6rem', bgcolor: issue.wcagLevel === 'APCA' ? MSQDX_BRAND_PRIMARY.purple : MSQDX_BRAND_PRIMARY.green, color: '#fff' }} /\u003e}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 1034,
            "text": "                                                        \u003c/Box\u003e"
          },

            "text": "                                                        "

          {

          {

          },
```

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I have a clear picture. Now I need to create a comprehensive plan that covers:

1. **Option A – Lightweight saliency (MSI-Net/ONNX)**
   - Add optional backend: either new service (second Docker image) or same service with env SALIENCY_BACKEND=sum|msinet. Research suggests ONNX + CoreML for Apple Silicon. For Coolify (likely Linux), ONNX Runtime on CPU. So we need:
   - New Python service or extend main_sum.py to support "msinet" path: load MSI-Net (or SimpleSalNet) via ONNX, same API (image in → heatmap PNG out). Or a separate Dockerfile.msinet.
   - CHECKION: env SALIENCY_BACKEND or second URL SALIENCY_MSINET_SERVICE_URL? Simpler: one service URL, backend chosen by env in the service. So deploy either SUM container or MSI-Net container; CHECKION just calls SALIENCY_SERVICE_URL. So the "lightweight" option is a separate deployment (Dockerfile.msinet + main_msinet.py) that exposes same /jobs and /predict API. Then we document: for CPU/low latency deploy MSI-Net image and point SALIENCY_SERVICE_URL to it.
   - Need to find or define MSI-Net/SimpleSalNet ONNX model. I'll plan: add saliency-service variant (MSI-Net via ONNX), same async job API, same fusion in CHECKION.

2. **Option B – Vision API**
   - Already have optional LLM Vision. Optional: add Claude or GCP Vision as alternative/additional. Plan: optional second provider (e.g. CLIENT_SALIENCY_VISION_PROVIDER=openai|claude|gcp), or just document that they can use OpenAI; Claude could be added later. Keep it minimal: document that Vision is optional and can be extended.

3. **Option C – Hybrid**
   - Already in place. MediaPipe: add optional face/gaze detection that outputs regions; feed into fusion as another prior. Plan: optional MediaPipe step (e.g. in Python service or separate Node script). If we add MediaPipe, it would run on the screenshot and produce "face regions" or "gaze regions" that we add to the fusion. So: new optional component that runs on screenshot, returns list of rects + scores, fusion in CHECKION adds them like vision regions.

4. **Scanpath (heuristic, no GPU)**
   - Algorithm: decode heatmap to intensity (we have decodeJetPngToIntensity); find local maxima (peaks); sort by saliency × position weight (F-pattern: top-left bias, or Z-pattern) × element size; inhibition-of-return (after picking a fixation, reduce saliency in a radius around it, then pick next). Output: ordered list of fixation points (x, y) or region IDs. We can map fixations to pageIndex regions by "which region contains this point". So scanpath = array of { x, y, regionId?, order }.
   - Where to compute: in Next.js after fusion (we have intensity array in memory when we do fusion) or in a small lib that takes heatmap + pageIndex and returns scanpath. So: new lib/scanpath.ts (or saliency-scanpath.ts) that: takes heatmap PNG base64 + pageIndex + dimensions; decodes to intensity; finds peaks (e.g. 2d local max with threshold); applies IoR and ordering; returns list of fixations. Then in result route after we have the fused heatmap, we call computeScanpath(heatmapBase64, pageIndex, width, height) and store on result (new field scanpath?: ScanpathFixation[]).
   - Type: ScanpathFixation = { x: number; y: number; regionId?: string; order: number } or similar. Store on ScanResult.

5. **Composite scoring**
   - Already have fusion. No change needed except possibly adding MediaPipe or GCP as additional signals.

6. **OpenVINO**
   - Only relevant if we run on Intel CPU in the saliency service. Plan: document that for Intel deployments, consider exporting SUM/MSI-Net to OpenVINO for 2-4x speedup; optional future improvement.

Let me structure the plan with todos. Scope for "plan everything":
- Phase 1: Scanpath heuristic (no new backend) – clear value, uses existing data.
- Phase 2: Lightweight saliency backend (MSI-Net/ONNX) – new Docker variant, same API.
- Phase 3: Optional MediaPipe face/gaze – optional prior in fusion.
- Phase 4: Optional second Vision provider (Claude/GCP) – config/code for multiple providers.

I'll create one plan that covers all, with explicit phases and file references.
</details></think>

```json
{
  "name": "Saliency Hybrid Scanpath Plan",
  "overview": "Plan zur Integration der recherchierten CPU-tauglichen Saliency-Strategien: (1) Scanpath-Heuristik aus Heatmap + DOM, (2) optionales leichtes Saliency-Backend (MSI-Net/ONNX), (3) optionale Erweiterungen (MediaPipe Face/Gaze, zweiter Vision-Provider). Die bestehende Fusion (SUM + DOM + optional LLM Vision) bleibt zentral; neue Komponenten bauen darauf auf.",
  "plan": "# Saliency Hybrid + Scanpath – Implementierungsplan\n\n## Ausgangslage\n\n- **Saliency:** SUM-Service (async Jobs), Fusion in Next.js: SUM-Heatmap + DOM (Rechteck-Füllung aus [lib/saliency-fusion.ts](lib/saliency-fusion.ts)) + optional LLM Vision ([lib/saliency-vision-cache.ts](lib/saliency-vision-cache.ts)).\n- **API:** [app/api/saliency/generate/route.ts](app/api/saliency/generate/route.ts) (POST → jobId oder AI sofort), [app/api/saliency/result/route.ts](app/api/saliency/result/route.ts) (GET → Fusion + Enrich + Update Scan).\n- **Typen:** [lib/types.ts](lib/types.ts) – &#96;ScanResult&#96; mit &#96;saliencyHeatmap&#96;, &#96;pageIndex&#96;; keine Scanpath-Struktur bisher.\n\n---\n\n## Phase 1: Scanpath-Heuristik (ohne GPU)\n\n**Ziel:** Aus der gefusionierten Heatmap eine plausible Blickreihenfolge ableiten (Fixationspunkte), ohne zusätzliches ML. Nutzt bestehende Daten (Heatmap + pageIndex).\n\n### Algorithmus\n\n1. **Intensität:** Gefusionierte Heatmap (PNG) mit bestehendem &#96;decodeJetPngToIntensity&#96; in [lib/saliency-fusion.ts](lib/saliency-fusion.ts) zu Float32-Intensität dekodieren.\n2. **Peaks:** Lokale Maxima finden (z.B. Fenster 5×5 oder 9×9, Schwellwert z.B. 0.1), oder alternativ pro &#96;pageIndex&#96;-Region den Pixel mit maximaler Saliency in der Region als Kandidat.\n3. **Sortierung:** Kandidaten bewerten mit  \n   &#96;score = saliency × positionWeight × sizeWeight&#96;  \n   - **positionWeight:** F-Pattern / Z-Pattern (z.B. &#96;1 / (1 + 0.3 * (y/height))&#96; für leichten Top-Bias, oder explizit oben/links bevorzugt).  \n   - **sizeWeight:** z.B. &#96;log(1 + area)&#96; oder Fläche der Region aus &#96;pageIndex.regions[].rect&#96;.\n4. **Inhibition-of-Return (IoR):** Nach Wahl eines Fixationspunkts einen Radius (z.B. 80–120 px) um diesen Punkt in der Intensitäts-Kopie abwerten (z.B. auf 0 oder linear abfallend), dann nächsten Peak wählen. Wiederholen bis N Fixationen (z.B. 5–10) oder keine Kandidaten mehr.\n5. **Ausgabe:** Geordnete Liste von Fixationen, optional mit Zuordnung zu &#96;pageIndex.regions&#96; (welche Region enthält (x,y)).\n\n### Datenmodell\n\n- **Neuer Typ** in [lib/types.ts](lib/types.ts): z.B.  \n  &#96;ScanpathFixation = { x: number; y: number; order: number; regionId?: string; saliency?: number }&#96;  \n  und  \n  &#96;ScanResult.scanpath?: ScanpathFixation[]&#96;\n- **Speicherung:** Scanpath nur setzen, wenn &#96;pageIndex&#96; und &#96;saliencyHeatmap&#96; vorhanden sind; Berechnung in der Result-Route nach der Fusion.\n\n### Implementierung\n\n- **Neues Modul** &#96;lib/scanpath.ts&#96; (oder &#96;lib/saliency-scanpath.ts&#96;):\n  - &#96;computeScanpath(options: { heatmapPngBase64: string; pageIndex: PageIndex; width: number; height: number; maxFixations?: number; iorRadius?: number }) =\u003e Promise\u003cScanpathFixation[]\u003e&#96;\n  - Darin: &#96;decodeJetPngToIntensity&#96; (aus saliency-fusion) aufrufen, Peaks finden (auf Intensitäts-Array), Bewertung + IoR in einer Schleife, optional Zuordnung zu Regionen (Punkt-in-Rechteck für &#96;pageIndex.regions&#96;).\n- **Integration** in [app/api/saliency/result/route.ts](app/api/saliency/result/route.ts): Nach &#96;fuseSaliencyHeatmap&#96; und &#96;enrichPageIndexWithSaliency&#96; die gefusionierte Heatmap-Base64 + &#96;result.pageIndex&#96; + Breite/Höhe an &#96;computeScanpath&#96; übergeben; Ergebnis in &#96;updateScanResult(..., { saliencyHeatmap, pageIndex, scanpath })&#96; schreiben.\n- **AI-Pfad** in [app/api/saliency/generate/route.ts](app/api/saliency/generate/route.ts): Nach dem gleichen Muster nach Fusion und Enrich den Scanpath berechnen und mit speichern.\n- **Frontend:** Optional neue UI auf der Ergebnisseite (z.B. Toggle „Scanpath anzeigen“), die &#96;result.scanpath&#96; als nummerierte Kreise/Punkte auf dem Screenshot overlayt (Reihenfolge 1, 2, 3, …). Dafür z.B. neue Komponente &#96;ScanpathOverlay&#96; oder Erweiterung von [app/results/[id]/page.tsx](app/results/[id]/page.tsx) / [components/PageIndexRegionsOverlay.tsx](components/PageIndexRegionsOverlay.tsx).\n\n### Konfiguration\n\n- Env oder Konstanten für &#96;maxFixations&#96; (z.B. 8), &#96;iorRadius&#96; (z.B. 100), Peak-Schwellwert – in &#96;lib/scanpath.ts&#96; mit sinnvollen Defaults; optional aus Env lesen.\n\n---\n\n## Phase 2: Leichtgewichtiges Saliency-Backend (MSI-Net / ONNX)\n\n**Ziel:** Optional ein zweites, CPU-freundliches Backend (1–3 s auf M-Chip), gleiche API wie SUM (Jobs + Heatmap-PNG), damit Coolify wahlweise SUM oder MSI-Net betreiben kann.\n\n### Architektur\n\n- **Zwei Deployment-Varianten:** Wie heute: entweder SUM-Image (Dockerfile.sum) oder neues **MSI-Net-Image** (z.B. &#96;Dockerfile.msinet&#96;). CHECKION ruft weiterhin &#96;SALIENCY_SERVICE_URL&#96; auf; kein zweiter URL-Parameter nötig.\n- **Gleiche API:**  \n  - &#96;POST /jobs&#96; mit &#96;image_base64&#96; → &#96;job_id&#96;  \n  - &#96;GET /jobs/{job_id}&#96; → &#96;status&#96;, bei &#96;completed&#96; → &#96;heatmap_base64&#96;  \n  So bleibt [app/api/saliency/generate/route.ts](app/api/saliency/generate/route.ts) und [app/api/saliency/result/route.ts](app/api/saliency/result/route.ts) unverändert bezüglich Aufruflogik; nur die Service-URL zeigt auf SUM oder MSI-Net.\n\n### Service-Seite (saliency-service)\n\n- **Neues Dockerfile** &#96;saliency-service/Dockerfile.msinet&#96;:  \n  - Python 3.10+, ONNX Runtime (CPU), ggf. keine schweren PyTorch/CUDA-Abhängigkeiten falls Modell nur ONNX ist.  \n  - Eigenes &#96;main_msinet.py&#96; (oder &#96;main_onnx.py&#96;): FastAPI, gleiche Endpoints wie [saliency-service/main_sum.py](saliency-service/main_sum.py) (&#96;POST /jobs&#96;, &#96;GET /jobs/{job_id}&#96;), optional weiterhin &#96;POST /predict&#96; für Rückwärtskompatibilität.  \n  - Modell: MSI-Net oder SimpleSalNet als ONNX-Modell (z.B. aus öffentlichen Repos exportieren oder vorkonvertiert ablegen). Gewichte/ONNX-Datei per Build-Arg oder Volume/Umgebungsvariable (z.B. &#96;MSINET_WEIGHTS_PATH&#96;), analog zu SUM-Weights.\n- **Inferenz:** Bild base64 → decode → Preprocessing (Resize, Normalisierung wie im Original-Modell) → ONNX Session run → Post-Processing (z.B. Resize auf Ausgabegröße, gleiche Jet/Colormap wie SUM). Optional [saliency-service/heatmap_postprocess.py](saliency-service/heatmap_postprocess.py) für EyeQuant-Style nutzen.\n- **Dokumentation:** In [docs/deployment/coolify-saliency-schritt-fuer-schritt.md](docs/deployment/coolify-saliency-schritt-fuer-schritt.md) Abschnitt „MSI-Net (leichtes Backend)“: Build mit &#96;Dockerfile.msinet&#96;, &#96;SALIENCY_SERVICE_URL&#96; auf diesen Container setzen; Hinweis auf 1–3 s Latenz und CPU-Tauglichkeit.\n\n### Recherche/Abhängigkeit\n\n- Konkrete ONNX-Modelle: MSI-Net/SimpleSalNet – Quellen (z.B. GitHub, Paper-Repos) ermitteln; falls nur PyTorch verfügbar, einmalig Export-Skript (PyTorch → ONNX) vorschlagen und ONNX-Datei im Repo oder über GDrive/URL bereitstellen.\n\n---\n\n## Phase 3: Optionale Erweiterungen\n\n### 3a) MediaPipe Face/Gaze (optional)\n\n- **Idee:** Screenshot an MediaPipe übergeben (Face Detection / Gaze), Rechtecke + ggf. Scores als weiteres „Prior“-Signal in die Fusion.\n- **Ort:** Entweder im Saliency-Service (Python: &#96;mediapipe&#96;) oder in Next.js (z.B. separater API-Route oder in generate/result). Ausgabe: Liste von &#96;{ rect, score }&#96; analog zu Vision-Regionen.\n- **Fusion:** In [lib/saliency-fusion.ts](lib/saliency-fusion.ts) eine weitere Prior-Map (z.B. &#96;buildMediaPipePriorMap&#96;) und optionalen Parameter &#96;mediaPipeRects?, mediaPipeWeight?&#96; in &#96;fuseSaliencyHeatmap&#96;; gleiche Normalisierung und Addition wie bei Vision.\n- **Konfiguration:** Env z.B. &#96;SALIENCY_MEDIAPIPE_ENABLED=true&#96;; nur wenn gesetzt, MediaPipe ausführen und Gewicht (z.B. 0.15) anwenden. Geringe Priorität, da „nice-to-have“.\n\n### 3b) Zweiter Vision-Provider (Claude / GCP) (optional)\n\n- **Idee:** Semantische AOI-Analyse nicht nur über OpenAI, sondern wahlweise Claude Vision oder Google Cloud Vision (Labels + Object Localization).\n- **Umsetzung:** Abstraktion „Vision-Provider“ in [lib/saliency-ai.ts](lib/saliency-ai.ts) (oder neues &#96;lib/saliency-vision-providers.ts&#96;): gemeinsames Interface &#96;getAttentionRegions(imageBase64, width, height) =\u003e Promise\u003cAttentionRegion[]\u003e&#96;. Implementierungen: &#96;openai&#96;, &#96;claude&#96;, &#96;gcp&#96;. Env z.B. &#96;SALIENCY_VISION_PROVIDER=openai|claude|gcp&#96; und entsprechende API-Keys. Bestehende OpenAI-Nutzung als Default beibehalten.\n- **Fusion:** Unverändert; alle Provider liefern dasselbe &#96;AttentionRegion&#96;-Format, das bereits in &#96;buildVisionPriorMap&#96; und im Vision-Cache verwendet wird.\n\n---\n\n## Phase 4: Dokumentation und Konfiguration\n\n- **Knowledge:** [knowledge/checkion-saliency-fusion.md](knowledge/checkion-saliency-fusion.md) um Scanpath, MSI-Net-Option und optionale Erweiterungen (MediaPipe, zweiter Vision-Provider) ergänzen.\n- **Coolify-Doku:** [docs/deployment/coolify-saliency-schritt-fuer-schritt.md](docs/deployment/coolify-saliency-schritt-fuer-schritt.md):  \n  - Scanpath: automatisch aktiv wenn Heatmap + pageIndex vorhanden; ggf. Env für &#96;maxFixations&#96;/&#96;iorRadius&#96;.  \n  - MSI-Net: Build \u0026 Deploy mit Dockerfile.msinet, &#96;SALIENCY_SERVICE_URL&#96; zeigen.  \n  - OpenVINO: Kurz erwähnen als mögliche spätere Optimierung für Intel-CPU (2–4× Speedup), ohne konkrete Implementierung.\n\n---\n\n## Abhängigkeiten und Reihenfolge\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;mermaid\nflowchart LR\n    subgraph phase1 [Phase 1]\n        A[decodeJetPngToIntensity]\n        B[Peaks + IoR + Sort]\n        C[computeScanpath]\n        D[ScanpathFixation type]\n        E[result route + generate route]\n        F[ScanpathOverlay UI]\n    end\n    subgraph phase2 [Phase 2]\n        G[Dockerfile.msinet]\n        H[main_msinet.py ONNX]\n        I[Doc]\n    end\n    subgraph phase3 [Phase 3 optional]\n        J[MediaPipe prior]\n        K[Vision provider abstraction]\n    end\n    A --\u003e B --\u003e C\n    D --\u003e C\n    C --\u003e E --\u003e F\n    G --\u003e H --\u003e I\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n- **Phase 1** ist unabhängig und bringt sofort Mehrwert (Scanpath aus bestehender Heatmap + DOM).\n- **Phase 2** erfordert Beschaffung/Export eines ONNX-Modells (MSI-Net/SimpleSalNet).\n- **Phase 3** kann nach Bedarf umgesetzt werden.\n\n---\n\n## Tests\n\n- **Scanpath:** Unit-Tests in &#96;lib/scanpath.test.ts&#96;: feste Heatmap (z.B. zwei getrennte Hotspots) + Mock-pageIndex; erwartete Reihenfolge und ungefähre Koordinaten prüfen; IoR prüfen (zweiter Peak nicht im Radius des ersten).\n- **Fusion:** Bestehende Tests in [lib/saliency-fusion.test.ts](lib/saliency-fusion.test.ts) beibehalten; bei MediaPipe-Erweiterung optionale Tests für &#96;buildMediaPipePriorMap&#96; bzw. erweiterte &#96;fuseSaliencyHeatmap&#96;-Aufrufe.\n- **MSI-Net:** Integrationstest im Service (optional): ein Testbild durchreichen, prüfen dass PNG zurückkommt und Dimensionen stimmen.\n\n---\n\n## Kurzfassung der betroffenen Dateien\n\n| Bereich | Dateien (neu/geändert) |\n|--------|--------------------------|\n| Scanpath | Neu: &#96;lib/scanpath.ts&#96;, &#96;lib/scanpath.test.ts&#96;; Typen in &#96;lib/types.ts&#96;; Integration in &#96;app/api/saliency/result/route.ts&#96;, &#96;app/api/saliency/generate/route.ts&#96;; optional UI in &#96;app/results/[id]/page.tsx&#96; + &#96;components/ScanpathOverlay.tsx&#96; |\n| MSI-Net | Neu: &#96;saliency-service/Dockerfile.msinet&#96;, &#96;saliency-service/main_msinet.py&#96;; Doku in &#96;docs/deployment/...&#96; |\n| MediaPipe / Vision | Optional: &#96;lib/saliency-fusion.ts&#96; (MediaPipe-Prior), &#96;lib/saliency-vision-providers.ts&#96; oder Erweiterung &#96;lib/saliency-ai.ts&#96; |\n| Doku | &#96;knowledge/checkion-saliency-fusion.md&#96;, &#96;docs/deployment/coolify-saliency-schritt-fuer-schritt.md&#96; |\n"
}
```

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

Now create lib/scanpath.ts with computeScanpath. It needs to:
1. Decode heatmap PNG (base64) to intensity using decodeJetPngToIntensity - but that takes Buffer, so we need to Buffer.from(base64, 'base64')
2. Find peaks: local maxima in intensity (e.g. 5x5 or 9x9 window, threshold 0.1). Or use pageIndex regions and take max pixel per region as candidate - plan says "or alternativ pro pageIndex-Region den Pixel mit maximaler Saliency in der Region als Kandidat". I'll do local maxima first (simpler and works without pageIndex for peak detection), then we can assign regionId by point-in-rect.
3. Score = saliency * positionWeight * sizeWeight. positionWeight: F-pattern 1/(1+0.3*(y/height)). sizeWeight: for peaks we don't have "region" at peak detection - we have pixel. So we could either (a) find peaks first then for each peak find which region contains it and use that region's area, or (b) use candidate points from each region (max saliency pixel in region). Plan says "oder alternativ pro pageIndex-Region den Pixel mit maximaler Saliency in der Region als Kandidat". So two approaches - (1) local maxima then sort by score with position/size from containing region, or (2) one candidate per region (max pixel in region). I'll go with (1): find local maxima, then for each peak get containing region (if any) for sizeWeight and regionId; positionWeight from y.
4. IoR: after picking a fixation, decay intensity in radius iorRadius (e.g. 100px), then pick next.
5. Return array of ScanpathFixation.

Let me implement. I'll need to get intensity as Float32Array, width, height. Then find local maxima: for each pixel (x,y) check if it's the max in a window (e.g. 5x5). Threshold: only consider if intensity > 0.1. So we get list of {x, y, saliency}. Then sort by score = saliency * positionWeight(y) * (sizeWeight from region or 1 if no region). Then IoR loop: pick best, add to result, decay in radius, repeat until maxFixations or no candidates left.

For position weight: F-pattern = top-left bias. positionWeight = 1 / (1 + 0.3 * (y/height)) so top has higher weight.
For size weight: if we have a containing region, area = rect.width * rect.height, sizeWeight = Math.log(1 + area) or similar. If no region, use 1.

Implementation details:
- decodeJetPngToIntensity expects Buffer. So: raw = heatmapPngBase64 (strip data URL if present), Buffer.from(raw, 'base64').
- Local maxima: window 5x5, threshold 0.1. For each pixel (x,y) where x in [2, w-3], y in [2, h-3], check if intensity[y*w+x] is max in the 5x5 and >= 0.1. Collect (x, y, saliency).
- Find containing region: for each peak (x,y), find first region in pageIndex.regions where rect contains (x,y): rect.x <= x < rect.x+rect.width && rect.y <= y < rect.y+rect.height. area = rect.width*rect.height, sizeWeight = Math.log(1 + area).
- Sort candidates by score = saliency * (1/(1+0.3*y/h)) * (region ? Math.log(1+area) : 1). Actually we need to do IoR so we don't sort once - we repeatedly pick the best candidate, then decay, then pick next. So: candidates array with {x, y, saliency, regionId?, sizeWeight}. Then loop: compute score for each (position weight * sizeWeight * saliency), pick max, push to result, then decay intensity in copy in radius iorRadius around (x,y), and remove or update candidates that are in that radius (or just recompute which candidate is best from remaining intensity). Simpler: keep a copy of intensity (float32), and each iteration compute all candidates' "effective" saliency from the current intensity (so after IoR the intensity is reduced), then score = effectiveSaliency * positionWeight * sizeWeight, pick max, add to result, decay intensity in radius. So we don't remove candidates - we just read intensity at (x,y) each time, and after we decay the intensity the same candidate will have lower value next time. Good.
- Default maxFixations 8, iorRadius 100.
</details></think>

Fix the import - we're in lib/ so @/lib/types might resolve. Let me check - in Next.js/ts @/ is usually src or project root. So @/lib/types is correct. But decodeJetPngToIntensity is from saliency-fusion - that might create a circular dependency if saliency-fusion imports something from scanpath. Let me check - saliency-fusion imports PageIndex from types and AttentionRegion from saliency-ai. It doesn't import scanpath. So we're good.

I had a small bug: we're not using the decoded w,h when dimensions might differ - we use W, H from decoded. So we're good. Let me remove the unused "if (w !== width || h !== height)" block or use it - actually we just use w,h as W,H so we're consistent. Good.

Now integrate in result route and generate route. Then add UI and tests.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

Now write lib/scanpath.test.ts. We need to test:
- computeScanpath with a fixed heatmap (e.g. two hotspots) + mock pageIndex; expect order and approximate coordinates
- IoR: second peak not in radius of first

To get a fixed heatmap we can use intensityToJetPng from saliency-fusion to create a small PNG with two bright spots, then decode and run computeScanpath. Or we can create a minimal PNG buffer (e.g. 100x80) with known hot pixels and run computeScanpath. The decodeJetPngToIntensity reads R channel - so we need a PNG where R is high at our peak locations. We can use sharp to create a raw RGB buffer (R=255 at peaks, 0 elsewhere), encode as PNG, then run computeScanpath. So: create 50x50 image, set pixel (10,10) and (35,30) to red (255,0,0), rest 0. Then encode to PNG, base64. pageIndex with two regions that contain these points. Then computeScanpath - we should get two fixations, order 1 and 2, and the first should be the one with higher position weight (top-left), so (10,10) should be order 1. Let me write the test.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

In findPeaks we require the center to be strictly greater than all neighbors. So for (15,15) we have value 0.9 and neighbors 0.5 - good. For (70,50) same. But we're also setting neighbors to 0.5 in a 5x5 - so the center 15,15 has 24 neighbors at 0.5, and center 70,50 same. So both are local maxima. However the peak window in scanpath is 5x5, so half=2. So we iterate y from 2 to h-3 = 77, x from 2 to w-3 = 97. So (15,15) and (70,50) are both in range. Good.

Run the test - we need to fix the test: the neighborhood we set might make the center not the only max in the 5x5 - we set all 24 neighbors to 0.5 and center to 0.9, so center is the max. Good.

Let me run the test.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

MSI-Net is Keras/TF, not ONNX. So we have two options: (1) Create a Python service that uses TensorFlow/Keras and MSI-Net from HuggingFace (heavier), or (2) Create an ONNX-based service that expects a pre-exported ONNX model (e.g. user exports from PyTorch or we document how to export). The plan says "MSI-Net via ONNX" and "ONNX Runtime – many PyTorch models can be exported to ONNX". So the plan expects a model that can run on ONNX. I'll create main_msinet.py that:
- Uses ONNX Runtime to load a .onnx file (path from env MSINET_WEIGHTS_PATH or similar)
- If no ONNX model is available, we could still provide the same API and return a placeholder or fail with a clear message. Better: implement the full pipeline with a placeholder/dummy model that just returns a gradient (so the API works) and document that users need to add a real ONNX saliency model. Or we use a simple fallback: e.g. numpy-based "saliency" (e.g. center bias + edge map) so the service runs without external weights - then document that for production they should use a real ONNX model.

Actually the plan says "Modell: MSI-Net oder SimpleSalNet als ONNX-Modell (z. B. aus öffentlichen Repos exportieren oder vorkonvertiert ablegen)." So we need to support loading an ONNX file. I'll create:
1. main_msinet.py - loads ONNX from path, preprocesses image (resize to model input size, normalize), runs session.run(), postprocesses output to heatmap (resize to orig size), applies heatmap_postprocess, jet colormap, returns PNG. Same job API.
2. Dockerfile.msinet - Python 3.10+, onnxruntime, Pillow, numpy, scipy, fastapi, uvicorn; copy heatmap_postprocess.py; no SUM repo. Model path via env.

For the ONNX model: input shape and output shape vary by model. A common saliency model input is e.g. 224x224 or 384x384. I'll use a configurable input size (e.g. 224) and assume output is a single-channel heatmap (same or smaller size). If no model file is present, we can fail at startup with a clear message.

Let me implement:
- requirements-msinet.txt: onnxruntime, pillow, numpy, scipy, fastapi, uvicorn
- main_msinet.py: load ONNX, decode image, resize to input size, normalize (ImageNet mean/std or 0-1), run, resize output to requested size, postprocess, jet, return base64
- Dockerfile.msinet: FROM python:3.11-slim, install requirements, copy main_msinet.py and heatmap_postprocess.py, ENV MSINET_MODEL_PATH=/app/model.onnx, document that user must provide the model
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I'll create a minimal ONNX-based service that:
1. Loads an ONNX model from a path (env MSINET_MODEL_PATH or /app/model.onnx). If the file doesn't exist, we still start but return 503 on predict until a model is provided (or we could fail at startup - clearer to fail at startup).
2. Preprocess: image -> resize to model input (e.g. 224x224), normalize (0-1 or ImageNet), NCHW.
3. Run ONNX session.
4. Postprocess: output tensor -> resize to requested out_w, out_h, apply heatmap_postprocess, jet colormap, PNG bytes.
5. Same job API as main_sum.

The tricky part is that different ONNX saliency models have different input/output shapes. I'll use a generic approach: assume input is e.g. "input" or "image" with shape [1, 3, H, W] and output is a single tensor with shape [1, 1, h, w] or [1, h, w]. We can make input size configurable via env (e.g. MSINET_INPUT_SIZE=224).

Let me create the files. If no ONNX model is in the repo, the Dockerfile can document that users must mount a volume or build with a model; the service will fail at startup with "Model file not found" which is acceptable.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The service will fail at startup if /app/model.onnx doesn't exist. For a better DX we could make the server start and return 503 until the model is present - but the plan says "Gewichte/ONNX-Datei per Build-Arg oder Volume/Umgebungsvariable". So we document that users must mount the model. I'll add a note in the Dockerfile that the container expects the model at /app/model.onnx or MSINET_MODEL_PATH. If we want the container to build and run without a model (e.g. for CI), we could add a stub that creates a dummy ONNX or we skip the load_model in lifespan when file is missing and return 503 - that way the container starts. Let me change load_model to not raise but set session=None when file missing, and in lifespan we don't call load_model if we want to allow no model - actually the plan says "Modell: MSI-Net oder SimpleSalNet als ONNX-Modell ... Gewichte/ONNX-Datei per Build-Arg oder Volume". So the deployment is expected to have the model. I'll keep the current behavior (fail at startup). The Coolify doc will explain to mount the volume or use a model URL.

Now Phase 3: Optional MediaPipe + Vision provider. The plan says "optional" and "can be done as needed". I'll add a minimal structure:
- For MediaPipe: add a placeholder or a small note in saliency-fusion that we could add buildMediaPipePriorMap - or actually implement a thin wrapper that calls a mediapipe API (would need to run in Node or Python). The plan says "Entweder im Saliency-Service (Python: mediapipe) oder in Next.js". So we could add a MediaPipe step in the saliency service (Python) that returns rects - but that would require a new endpoint. Simpler: add in knowledge/docs that MediaPipe can be added later; optionally add an interface type and a no-op or stub in fusion for mediaPipeRects. I'll add the fusion option (mediaPipeRects, mediaPipeWeight) and a stub buildMediaPipePriorMap that returns zeros if no rects, so the API is there; the actual MediaPipe detection would be a separate service or route. Actually the plan says "Geringe Priorität, da nice-to-have". I'll add the types and one optional parameter in FuseSaliencyOptions so it's extensible, and a short comment. No full MediaPipe implementation.
- For Vision provider: add a simple provider abstraction in saliency-ai or a new file that has getAttentionRegions as interface and openai as default implementation; claude/gcp can be stubs. That way the structure is there.

Let me do a minimal Phase 3:
1. In saliency-fusion: add optional mediaPipeRects?: { rect: {x,y,width,height}; score: number }[], mediaPipeWeight?: number, and in fuseSaliencyHeatmap if present call buildMediaPipePriorMap (we need to define that - same as vision, Gaussian blobs from rects). So buildMediaPipePriorMap(rects, width, height) and add to combined. I'll add the function and the option.
2. Vision provider: in lib/saliency-ai.ts we already have getAttentionRegionsFromVision (OpenAI). Add SALIENCY_VISION_PROVIDER env and a wrapper that calls OpenAI or a stub for claude/gcp that throws "not implemented". So we only add the env and a conditional; no need for a full abstraction unless we implement Claude. I'll add a comment and the env reading; keep existing OpenAI as default.

Actually re-reading the plan: "Optional MediaPipe + Vision provider structure" - so add the *structure* (interfaces, optional params), not necessarily full implementation. So:
- Fusion: add mediaPipeRects and mediaPipeWeight to FuseSaliencyOptions; add buildMediaPipePriorMap(rects, w, h) that takes {rect, score}[] and returns Float32Array (same logic as vision blobs). Then in fuseSaliencyHeatmap add the block for mediaPipe. So we need to implement buildMediaPipePriorMap (same as buildVisionPriorMap but with rect + score instead of left_pct etc). So it's a small addition.
- Vision: add getAttentionRegionsFromVisionProvider() that reads SALIENCY_VISION_PROVIDER and calls OpenAI or returns [] for others. So we just need to add the env check and maybe a type; keep existing function as is and add a wrapper that checks env.

Let me implement the fusion part for MediaPipe (rects with score) and the vision provider env check. I'll add a minimal MediaPipe prior map builder that takes rects in pixel coords + score.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Die Implementierung ist fertig. Der Build bricht ab, weil die lokalen Pakete `@msqdx/react` und `@msqdx/tokens` (file:../msqdx-design-system/...) im aktuellen Umfeld nicht aufgelöst werden – das ist ein bestehendes Setup-Problem, nicht durch die Änderungen verursacht.

Kurzüberblick:

---

## Erledigt

### Phase 1: Scanpath-Heuristik
- **Typen:** `ScanpathFixation` und `ScanResult.scanpath` in [lib/types.ts](lib/types.ts).
- **Modul:** [lib/scanpath.ts](lib/scanpath.ts) mit `computeScanpath` (Peaks, F-Pattern, IoR, Regionen-Zuordnung).
- **Anbindung:** Scanpath-Berechnung in [app/api/saliency/result/route.ts](app/api/saliency/result/route.ts) (SUM-Pfad) und [app/api/saliency/generate/route.ts](app/api/saliency/generate/route.ts) (AI-Pfad); Ergebnis wird beim Update des Scan-Results mitgespeichert.
- **UI:** [components/ScanpathOverlay.tsx](components/ScanpathOverlay.tsx), Toggle „Scanpath anzeigen/ausblenden“ im Visual-Tab der Ergebnisseite; i18n in [locales/en.json](locales/en.json) und [locales/de.json](locales/de.json).
- **Tests:** [lib/scanpath.test.ts](lib/scanpath.test.ts) und Script `npm run test:scanpath` – beide Tests laufen durch.

### Phase 2: MSI-Net/ONNX-Backend
- [saliency-service/main_msinet.py](saliency-service/main_msinet.py): FastAPI mit `/jobs`, `GET /jobs/{job_id}`, optional `/predict`; ONNX-Inferenz, gleiche Post-Processing-Pipeline wie SUM.
- [saliency-service/Dockerfile.msinet](saliency-service/Dockerfile.msinet) und [saliency-service/requirements-msinet.txt](saliency-service/requirements-msinet.txt).
- Modellpfad über `MSINET_MODEL_PATH`, optional `MSINET_INPUT_SIZE`; Container startet nur, wenn die ONNX-Datei existiert.

### Phase 3: Optionale Erweiterungen
- **MediaPipe:** In [lib/saliency-fusion.ts](lib/saliency-fusion.ts): `MediaPipeRect`, `buildMediaPipePriorMap`, sowie `mediaPipeRects` und `mediaPipeWeight` in `FuseSaliencyOptions` und in der Fusion.
- **Vision-Provider:** In [lib/saliency-ai.ts](lib/saliency-ai.ts): `SALIENCY_VISION_PROVIDER`; nur `openai` implementiert, `claude`/`gcp` liefern aktuell leere Regionen.

### Phase 4: Dokumentation
- [knowledge/checkion-saliency-fusion.md](knowledge/checkion-saliency-fusion.md): Scanpath, MSI-Net/ONNX, MediaPipe, Vision-Provider, OpenVINO-Hinweis, Tests.
- [docs/deployment/coolify-saliency-schritt-fuer-schritt.md](docs/deployment/coolify-saliency-schritt-fuer-schritt.md): Abschnitt „MSI-Net / ONNX“, Abschnitt „Scanpath (Blickreihenfolge)“.

---

Zum Build: Sobald `@msqdx/react` und `@msqdx/tokens` (z. B. durch `npm install` im Repo inkl. MSQDX-Design-System) verfügbar sind, sollte der Build mit den neuen Änderungen durchlaufen.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

On line 863 we have `<PageIndexCard pageIndex={data.pageIndex} ... />`. PageIndexCard does `const regions = Array.isArray(pageIndex?.regions) ? pageIndex.regions : [];` - so it's safe.

The issue might be that `data` from the API could have a different shape - e.g. when the scan result is stored in DB, sometimes `pageIndex` might be serialized in a way that `regions` becomes an object (e.g. keyed by id) instead of array. Let me check where the share page gets `data` from and what shape it has.

Actually re-reading the error: "r.filter is not a function" - so something named `r` doesn't have filter. In the share page we have `data.pageIndex.regions.map((r) =>` - so we're calling .map on regions. If regions is not an array, we'd get "regions.map is not a function" or "r.map is not a function" depending on minification. So the variable that's .filter - could be in a different component. 

Let me check - in PageIndexRegionsOverlay the flow is: safeRegions.filter((r) => r?.rect). So we call filter on safeRegions. If for some reason the component receives regions as a non-array but we have safeRegions = Array.isArray(regions) ? regions : []. If regions is an object, Array.isArray(regions) is false, so safeRegions = []. Then [].filter(...) works. So the only way we get r.filter is not a function is if somewhere the code does something like: let r = something; r.filter(...) and "something" is not an array. So maybe in the build, the variable that holds the array got minified to "r" and somewhere we're passing a non-array. For example in ShareSingleContent or similar, we might have const r = data.pageIndex?.regions; ... r.filter. So we need to ensure every place that uses regions from API/result defensively uses Array.isArray.

Let me search for "regions" or "pageIndex" used in a way that could lead to .filter:

_**Agent (default)**_


Hier die Änderungen und die Einordnung:

---

## 1. **`TypeError: r.filter is not a function`**

Ursache: An einigen Stellen wurde `.filter()` bzw. `.length` auf `result.issues` bzw. `data.issues` verwendet. Wenn die API oder die DB `issues` als Objekt, `undefined` oder `null` liefert (z.B. bei alten Daten oder anderen Serialisierungen), ist das kein Array und `.filter` schlägt fehl.

**Anpassungen:**

- **`app/results/[id]/page.tsx`**
  - Einheitliche Nutzung einer abgesicherten Variable:  
    `const issues = Array.isArray(result?.issues) ? result.issues : [];`
  - `filteredIssues` basiert jetzt auf `issues` (nicht mehr direkt auf `result.issues`).
  - Beim Berechnen der Level-Statistik:  
    `const dataIssues = Array.isArray(data?.issues) ? data.issues : [];` und dann `dataIssues.forEach(...)`.
  - Die Tab-Anzeige nutzt `issues.length` statt `result.issues.length`.

- **`app/share/[token]/page.tsx`** (ShareSingleContent)
  - `const dataIssues = Array.isArray(data?.issues) ? data.issues : [];`
  - `issueCount = dataIssues.length`
  - Beim Rendern der Issues: `dataIssues.slice(0, 50).map(...)` statt `(data.issues ?? []).slice(...)`.

Damit wird überall nur dann mit einem Array gearbeitet, wenn es wirklich ein Array ist; sonst wird ein leeres Array verwendet. Der Fehler `r.filter is not a function` sollte damit verschwinden.

---

## 2. **404 bei `/api/share/by-resource?type=single&id=...`**

Ein **404** von dieser Route ist vorgesehen, wenn für die angegebene Ressource (z.B. Scan `8e1fb7a2-...`) **noch kein Share-Link** existiert.

- **SharePanel** behandelt das bereits: bei 404 wird `setShareInfo(null)` aufgerufen und der Button „Teilen“ / „Share“ angezeigt.
- Die Meldung **„Failed to load resource: 404“** im DevTools-Netzwerk/Console kommt vom Browser für jede 404-Antwort. Das ist normales Verhalten und kein App-Fehler.

Wenn du möchtest, dass der 404 seltener oder gar nicht mehr auftritt, müsste man z.B. nur dann `/api/share/by-resource` aufrufen, wenn der Nutzer den Share-Bereich öffnet, statt beim Laden der Seite. Die aktuelle Logik (beim Laden prüfen, ob schon ein Share existiert) ist aber in Ordnung; der 404 ist dabei erwartbar.

---

**Kurz:** Der Fehler `r.filter is not a function` ist durch die Absicherung von `issues` behoben. Der 404 von `/api/share/by-resource` ist erwartbar, wenn noch kein Share existiert, und wird im UI bereits korrekt verarbeitet.

---

_**User**_

schau mal die heatmap sieht noch nicht wirklich rund aus, können wir ggf. sum analyse temporär deaktivieren. und schauen das die ganze seite analysiert wird. unten ist gerade sehr viel grau.  gibt es ausserdem einen guten algorythmus um kontraste der einzelnen elemente zueinander zu berechnen und daraus ebenfalls bewertungen für unsere attention heatmap zu erzeugen

---

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user has several requests:

1. **Heatmap doesn't look "round"** - they want to temporarily disable SUM analysis and see if the whole page gets analyzed (currently there's a lot of gray at the bottom - suggesting the heatmap/saliency might not cover the full page or SUM is biasing toward top-center).

2. **Ensure the whole page is analyzed** - "unten ist gerade sehr viel grau" (a lot of gray at the bottom) - so we need to make sure saliency/heatmap covers the full viewport/page.

3. **Contrast algorithm** - they're asking for a good algorithm to compute contrast between elements and use that to generate attention/heatmap scores.

Let me break this down:

**1. Temporarily disable SUM**
- There should be an env var or flag to skip calling the SUM service and only use structure (pageIndex) + optional vision. I need to find where the saliency flow decides to call SUM vs AI vs nothing, and add a way to skip SUM (e.g. `SALIENCY_USE_SUM=false` or similar).
- Alternatively we could make the fusion use only structure when SUM is disabled.

**2. Full-page analysis**
- The gray at the bottom could be because: (a) SUM model only outputs a certain resolution or crop, (b) the heatmap image is being rendered at wrong dimensions, (c) the fusion only fills where SUM has data. I need to check how the heatmap is built and ensure we're covering the full screenshot dimensions. If SUM is disabled, we'd rely on structure (pageIndex) which should cover all regions on the page - so ensuring pageIndex has full coverage and that we render the structure map at full size should help.

**3. Contrast-based attention**
- Contrast (e.g. color/luminance difference between an element and its background) is a known attention driver. We could:
  - Compute per-element contrast (e.g. from screenshot or from DOM/CSS) and add it as another prior in the fusion.
  - Simple approach: for each pageIndex region, estimate or get contrast (e.g. from computed styles or by sampling pixels in the screenshot). Then add a "contrast weight" to the structure map or a new layer in the fusion.
  - Algorithm: luminance contrast (WCAG-style) or color difference. We'd need either screenshot pixel data per region or headless browser computed styles. In the current stack we have screenshot as base64 and pageIndex with rects - so we could sample pixels in each rect and compute average luminance / contrast vs. surrounding.

I'll search the codebase for:
- Where SUM is invoked and how to disable it
- How heatmap dimensions are set (full page vs partial)
- Where fusion happens so we can add contrast
</details></think>

I need to:
1. Add a way to disable SUM (e.g. SALIENCY_USE_SUM=false) so we only use structure (+ optional vision). When SUM is disabled, we still need to produce a heatmap - we can build it from structure only (and optionally vision), covering the full page dimensions.
2. Ensure full-page coverage - when SUM is disabled, the heatmap will be 100% from structure map which is built at (width, height) of the screenshot, so it should cover the whole page. The issue with "gray at bottom" might be that SUM returns a smaller or cropped map that we're blending in. So disabling SUM and using only structure should fix the coverage.
3. Add a contrast-based attention algorithm: we need to compute contrast for each region. Options:
   - From screenshot: we'd need to decode the image and sample pixels in each pageIndex region, compute luminance (e.g. 0.299*R+0.599*G+0.114*B), then compare to background (e.g. surrounding area or parent). High contrast → higher attention.
   - Simpler: we could add a "contrast" field to the structure map by estimating from region size and position (e.g. larger elements or elements with more contrast to neighbors get higher weight). Without pixel access we could use heuristics (e.g. text regions vs images from semanticType).
   - Best approach: use sharp to get raw pixel buffer, for each region rect sample mean luminance inside rect and in a border around it, compute contrast ratio (e.g. Michelson or Weber), then add as a weight to the structure map.

Let me read more of the generate route to see the SUM path and how we build the heatmap when we have SUM result. Then I'll add SALIENCY_USE_SUM (or SALIENCY_SKIP_SUM) and a full-page structure-only path. For contrast I'll need to add a function that takes screenshot buffer + pageIndex and returns per-region contrast scores, then feed that into the structure map or a new "contrast" prior map.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

Now I understand the flow:

1. **Disable SUM**: I'll add `SALIENCY_USE_SUM` (default true). When false:
   - In generate: don't call the SUM service; instead build a heatmap from structure only (and optional vision). We need a path that creates a "structure-only" heatmap at full page dimensions and returns success (no jobId polling).
   - So we need a "structure-only" mode that: takes screenshot dimensions + pageIndex, builds structure map (and optionally vision), renders to Jet PNG, saves to scan, computes scanpath. All in one request (synchronous like AI path).

2. **Full page**: When SUM is disabled, the heatmap is 100% from our structure map which is built at (width, height) of the screenshot - so it's full page. The gray at the bottom with SUM is likely because SUM returns a map that doesn't cover the full image (e.g. different aspect or crop). So disabling SUM and using structure-only will naturally cover the whole page.

3. **Contrast algorithm**: 
   - We have screenshot as base64 and pageIndex with rects. We can use sharp to get raw pixels (RGB), then for each region compute mean luminance inside the rect and in a thin border (background). Contrast = difference or ratio (e.g. Weber contrast or Michelson). Then we can add this as a weight to the structure map or a separate "contrast" layer in the fusion.
   - Luminance: L = 0.299*R + 0.587*G + 0.114*B (relative luminance).
   - For each region: mean L inside rect, mean L in a 1px or 2px border around it (or surrounding area). Contrast could be (L_foreground - L_background) / (L_background + epsilon) or max(L_f, L_b) / (min(L_f, L_b) + epsilon). Normalize and use as a boost for that region in the structure map.
   - I'll add a function that takes (imageBuffer, pageIndex) and returns a Float32Array contrast map (same size as image), or per-region contrast scores that we merge into buildStructureAttentionMap. Easier: compute per-region contrast and multiply the structure weight by (1 + contrastScore) so high-contrast elements get higher attention.

Let me implement:

1. **Constants / env**: Add `SALIENCY_USE_SUM` - when false, we skip SUM and use structure-only (and optional vision). I'll use `SALIENCY_USE_SUM` default true so existing behavior is unchanged; set to 'false' to disable SUM.

2. **Generate route**: If !USE_SUM_SALIENCY, then:
   - Build heatmap from structure only: create a structure map at (imgWidth, imgHeight), optionally add vision map, normalize, render to Jet PNG, save to scan, scanpath, return success. No jobId.

3. **Fusion**: We need a way to produce a heatmap when there's no SUM input. So add a new function e.g. `renderStructureOnlyHeatmap({ pageIndex, width, height, visionRegions?, contrastFromImage? })` that builds structure map (+ optional vision), optionally applies contrast boost from image, then encodes to Jet PNG. The fusion module currently always expects sumHeatmapPngBase64 - so when SUM is disabled we don't call fuseSaliencyHeatmap with a SUM heatmap; we need a path that builds the heatmap from scratch. So:
   - In saliency-fusion.ts add `renderStructureOnlyHeatmap(options)` that builds structure map, optionally vision map, optionally contrast map, combines them, normalizes, returns base64 PNG.
   - Contrast: add `buildContrastAttentionMap(imageBuffer, pageIndex, width, height)` that samples pixels per region and returns a Float32Array (same dimensions) with contrast-based weights (or we could return per-region multipliers and apply in buildStructureAttentionMap). I'll do a contrast map that we can add as another layer: for each pixel we're in some region, use that region's contrast score; or we build a map where each region's rect is filled with its contrast score. So: compute per-region contrast (mean L inside vs mean L in border), then fill that region in the map with the contrast value. Then in renderStructureOnlyHeatmap we combine structure + contrast map.

4. **Contrast implementation**: 
   - get image as raw buffer (sharp: .raw() gives RGB)
   - for each pageIndex.regions with rect:
     - sample pixels in rect (x0..x1, y0..y1), compute mean R,G,B then L_inside = 0.299*R+...
     - sample pixels in border (e.g. expand rect by 2px and take the outer ring, or take a 2px band outside), mean L_border
     - contrast = |L_inside - L_border| / (L_border + 0.001) or use Weber; or (max - min) / (max + min) Michelson. Normalize to 0..1.
   - build a map same size as image, fill each region's rect with that contrast value (or with a boost factor). Then combine with structure map.

Let me read the types for PageIndexRegion to get rect structure.
</details></think>

1. **SALIENCY_USE_SUM** – when false, skip SUM entirely. In generate we build a structure-only heatmap at full screenshot dimensions and return success (no job). So we need:
   - A function that can produce a heatmap from structure (+ optional vision + optional contrast) without any SUM input. In fusion we have `fuseSaliencyHeatmap` which always expects sumHeatmapPngBase64. So I'll add a new function `renderStructureOnlyHeatmap` that:
     - Takes pageIndex, width, height, optional visionRegions, optional imageBuffer for contrast
     - Builds structure map at (width, height) - full page
     - Optionally adds vision map
     - Optionally builds contrast map from image and adds it
     - Normalizes, encodes to Jet PNG, returns base64
   - In generate route: if SALIENCY_USE_SUM === 'false', don't call SUM; call renderStructureOnlyHeatmap with full dimensions (imgWidth, imgHeight), then save, scanpath, return success.

2. **Contrast algorithm**: Add `buildContrastAttentionMap(imageBuffer, pageIndex, width, height)` in saliency-fusion.ts. It will:
   - Use sharp to get raw RGB buffer (we need to ensure dimensions match - the image is the screenshot)
   - For each region with rect: extract pixels in the rect, compute mean luminance (0.299*R+0.587*G+0.114*B). Extract pixels in a border (e.g. expand rect by 2px, take the frame pixels or a band outside). Mean luminance of border = background. Contrast = |L_foreground - L_background| / (L_background + 1e-3) or use (L_max - L_min)/(L_max + L_min). Normalize to 0..1 and fill the region in a Float32Array with that value.
   - Return the Float32Array map.

   Actually for sharp we get raw buffer - it's width*height*channels (3 for RGB). So we need to be careful with coordinates. Let me implement: sharp gives us buffer; we need width and height. For each region rect (in pixel coords), we sample pixels. Pixel at (x,y) is at index (y*width + x)*3 for R,G,B. We'll compute mean L inside rect and mean L in border (pixels that are in the expanded rect but not in the inner rect). Then contrast score for that region. Then we need to add this to the structure map - we could multiply structure weight by (1 + contrastFactor) per region, or add contrast as a separate layer. I'll add it as a separate layer: buildContrastAttentionMap returns a map same size as image; in renderStructureOnlyHeatmap we do combined = structureMap + structureWeight + contrastMap * contrastWeight. Actually the fusion currently does: combined = sumIntensity + structureWeight * structureMap + visionWeight * visionMap. So for structure-only we have no sumIntensity. So we need: combined = structureMap * weight + visionMap * visionWeight + contrastMap * contrastWeight, then normalize. Let me add contrast as an optional layer with a weight (e.g. 0.3).

3. **Full page**: When SUM is disabled, we use imgWidth and imgHeight from the screenshot metadata - so the heatmap is exactly the size of the full screenshot. So the whole page is covered.

Let me implement. First add the env var and the structure-only path in generate, then add contrast in saliency-fusion.

1. In lib/saliency-fusion.ts:
   - Add buildContrastAttentionMap(imageBuffer, pageIndex, width, height) - uses sharp to get raw RGB, for each region computes mean luminance inside rect and in border, contrast ratio, fills a Float32Array.
   - Add renderStructureOnlyHeatmap(options) - builds structure map, optionally contrast map, optionally vision map, combines, normalizes, returns base64 PNG. Options: pageIndex, width, height, visionRegions?, imageBuffer?, structureWeight, visionWeight, contrastWeight.

2. In app/api/saliency/generate/route.ts:
   - USE_SUM_SALIENCY = process.env.SALIENCY_USE_SUM !== 'false' (default true)
   - If !USE_SUM_SALIENCY and we have pageIndex: call renderStructureOnlyHeatmap with full image dimensions and image buffer for contrast, then save to scan, enrich, scanpath, return success (no jobId).
   - If !USE_SUM_SALIENCY and no pageIndex: we could still build a contrast-only or empty heatmap - or return an error that structure-only needs pageIndex. I'll require pageIndex for structure-only.

3. Constants: add SALIENCY_FUSION_CONTRAST_WEIGHT in docs.

Let me implement buildContrastAttentionMap. We need to get raw pixels. In Node we have the image as base64/buffer. sharp(buf).raw() gives us the raw buffer; we need to ensure we don't resize so dimensions stay width x height. So:
  const { data, info } = await sharp(imageBuffer).ensureAlpha().raw().toBuffer({ resolveWithObject: true });
  Actually ensureAlpha might change channels. Let me use .raw() without ensureAlpha - then we get RGB, 3 channels. info has width, height.
  For each region with rect (x, y, width, height) in pixel coords:
    - Clamp rect to image bounds
    - Sample all pixels in rect: for each pixel get R,G,B at (y*width+x)*3, compute L = 0.299*R + 0.587*G + 0.114*B, sum and count
    - Border: pixels that are in (rect.x-2, rect.y-2, rect.width+4, rect.height+4) but not in (rect.x, rect.y, rect.width, rect.height). Or we could take a 2px band outside the rect. Let me take inner rect and outer rect (expanded by 2px), border = outer - inner.
    - Mean L_inside, mean L_border. Contrast: Michelson (L_max - L_min)/(L_max + L_min) or simple ratio. I'll use relative difference: contrast = min(1, |L_inside - L_border| / (L_border + 0.01)). So high contrast when foreground is very different from background.
    - Fill the output map for that region's pixels with this contrast value (or with a normalized 0-1 value).
  Then normalize the whole map to 0-1.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

Now I need to add the structure-only branch. It should run when !USE_SUM_SALIENCY and result.pageIndex exists. We have imageBase64 and buf (we get buf later in the SUM path). We need to get buf and dimensions before the SUM path - we already have that in the AI path (buf, meta, width, height). So after the AI path block, before the "SUM path" block, add:

  // --- Structure-only path: no SUM, full-page heatmap from DOM + contrast ---
  if (!USE_SUM_SALIENCY && result.pageIndex) {
    try {
      const buf = Buffer.from(imageBase64, 'base64');
      const meta = await sharp(buf).metadata();
      const imgWidth = meta.width ?? 1920;
      const imgHeight = meta.height ?? 1080;
      let heatmapBase64 = await renderStructureOnlyHeatmap({
        pageIndex: result.pageIndex,
        width: imgWidth,
        height: imgHeight,
        structureWeight: 1,
        visionRegions: USE_LLM_REGIONS && process.env.OPENAI_API_KEY?.trim() ? await getAttentionRegionsFromVision(imageBase64, imgWidth, imgHeight).catch(() => []) : undefined,
        visionWeight: 0.2,
        imageBuffer: buf,
        contrastWeight: CONTRAST_WEIGHT,
      });
      const dataUrl = `data:image/png;base64,${heatmapBase64}`;
      let pageIndexPatch = {};
      try {
        pageIndexPatch.pageIndex = await enrichPageIndexWithSaliency(result.pageIndex, dataUrl);
      } catch (_) {}
      let scanpathPatch = {};
      try {
        const scanpath = await computeScanpath({ heatmapPngBase64: heatmapBase64, pageIndex: result.pageIndex, width: imgWidth, height: imgHeight });
        if (scanpath.length > 0) scanpathPatch.scanpath = scanpath;
      } catch (_) {}
      const updated = await updateScanResult(scanId, session.user.id, { saliencyHeatmap: dataUrl, ...pageIndexPatch, ...scanpathPatch });
      if (!updated) return NextResponse.json({ error: 'Failed to save heatmap to scan.' }, { status: 500 });
      invalidateScan(scanId);
      return NextResponse.json({ success: true });
    } catch (e) {
      console.error('[CHECKION] saliency/generate (structure-only):', e);
      return NextResponse.json({ error: e instanceof Error ? e.message : 'Structure-only heatmap failed' }, { status: 502 });
    }
  }

  if (!USE_SUM_SALIENCY) {
    return NextResponse.json(
      { error: 'Structure-only saliency requires pageIndex. Run a scan that produces structure map first.' },
      { status: 400 }
    );
  }

Actually if we're not using SUM and we don't have pageIndex, we should return a clear error. And the structure-only block should be right after the AI path, and we need to get imgWidth/imgHeight - they're computed in the SUM path with buf. So let me add the structure-only block after the AI path and use the same buf/meta that we'll need for the SUM path. So the flow is:
1. Get result, imageBase64
2. If USE_AI_SALIENCY -> AI path, return
3. If !USE_SUM_SALIENCY && result.pageIndex -> structure-only path, return
4. If !USE_SUM_SALIENCY -> return 400 (need pageIndex)
5. SUM path (create job, return jobId)

We need buf and meta for structure-only. So at the start of the structure-only block we do buf = Buffer.from(imageBase64, 'base64'), meta = await sharp(buf).metadata(), etc. Let me insert this block between the AI path and the SUM path.
</details></think>

  const jobId = createData.jobId;

So when we return { success: true } without jobId, the client will throw "heatmapError". We need to handle the case when the response has success: true but no jobId - in that case the heatmap is already done, so we should refetch the scan and update the result (or the API could return heatmapDataUrl in the response so the client can set it directly). The cleanest is to return heatmapDataUrl in the structure-only response and have the client check: if (createData.success && createData.heatmapDataUrl) then set result with the new heatmap and don't poll. I see - the client already handles createData.success: when createData.success is true it refetches the scan and updates the result. So we're good! The client checks `if (createData.success)` and then refetches. Perfect.

Now fix the contrast map: sharp().raw() without ensureAlpha may return 3 channels for a PNG that has no alpha. So we need to use the channels from info. I already have `channels = result.info.channels ?? 3`. So the getL function uses `(y * imgW + x) * channels` - good. Let me verify the getL indexing: for pixel (x,y), the index in raw is (y * imgW + x) * channels, and we want R=raw[i], G=raw[i+1], B=raw[i+2]. Good.

Let me run linter and add the env vars to the knowledge doc and deployment doc.

_**Agent (default)**_

Hier die Änderungen im Überblick:

---

## 1. SUM temporär deaktivieren

- **Env:** `SALIENCY_USE_SUM=false` (in Coolify bei der CHECKION-App setzen).
- **Verhalten:** Es wird **kein** SUM-Service aufgerufen. Die Heatmap wird nur aus **Struktur (pageIndex)** und **Kontrast** berechnet, in der **vollen Screenshot-Auflösung** – damit verschwindet das Grau unten, die ganze Seite wird abgedeckt.
- **Ablauf:** Synchron wie beim AI-Pfad: `POST /api/saliency/generate` liefert bei Erfolg `{ success: true }`, der Client lädt den Scan neu und zeigt die neue Heatmap. Kein Job, kein Polling.

---

## 2. Kontrast-Algorithmus für die Attention-Heatmap

- **Idee:** Pro **pageIndex-Region** wird aus dem Screenshot die **mittlere Luminanz** im Rechteck und in einem **2px-Rand** (Hintergrund) berechnet.
- **Kontrast:** Michelson-ähnlich:  
  `(L_max - L_min) / (L_max + L_min)`  
  Große Helligkeitsunterschiede (z. B. Text auf Hintergrund, CTAs) ergeben höhere Werte.
- **Einarbeitung:** Die Kontrast-Map wird mit dem Gewicht `SALIENCY_FUSION_CONTRAST_WEIGHT` (Default `0.25`) in die Struktur-only-Heatmap gemischt.
- **Code:** `buildContrastAttentionMap()` und Nutzung in `renderStructureOnlyHeatmap()` in `lib/saliency-fusion.ts`.

---

## 3. Was du in Coolify machen kannst

1. **CHECKION-App** → **Environment Variables**
2. **Neue Variable:**  
   - Name: `SALIENCY_USE_SUM`  
   - Wert: `false`
3. Optional:  
   - `SALIENCY_FUSION_CONTRAST_WEIGHT` = `0.25` (oder z. B. `0.3` für mehr Kontrast-Einfluss)
4. **Redeploy** der CHECKION-App.

Voraussetzung: Der Scan muss **pageIndex** liefern (Struktur-Map vom Scanner). Dann wird die Heatmap nur aus DOM-Struktur + Kontrast erzeugt und deckt die gesamte Seite ab.

---

## 4. Tests & Doku

- **Tests:** In `lib/saliency-fusion.test.ts` sind Tests für `buildContrastAttentionMap` und `renderStructureOnlyHeatmap` ergänzt; `npm run test:saliency-fusion` läuft durch.
- **Doku:**  
  - `knowledge/checkion-saliency-fusion.md`: SUM abstellbar, Kontrast, neue Env-Variablen.  
  - `docs/deployment/coolify-saliency-schritt-fuer-schritt.md`: Hinweis zu `SALIENCY_USE_SUM` und `SALIENCY_FUSION_CONTRAST_WEIGHT`.
